**Slovenia**,[[a]](#cite_note-12) officially the **Republic of Slovenia**,[[b]](#cite_note-15) is a country in [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe").[[13]](#cite_note-16)[[14]](#cite_note-17)[[15]](#cite_note-18)[[16]](#cite_note-19) It borders [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") to the west, [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") to the north, [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") to the northeast, and [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") to the south and southeast; its southwestern boundary consists of a 46.6 km (29.0 mi) coastline on the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea").[[17]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica_2021-20) Slovenia is mostly mountainous and forested,[[18]](#cite_note-Perko2004-21) covers 20,271 square kilometres (7,827 sq mi),[[19]](#cite_note-22) and has a population of approximately 2.1 million people.[[20]](#cite_note-23) [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language") is the official language.[[21]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica-24) Slovenia has a predominantly temperate [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate"),[[22]](#cite_note-Fallon2007-25) with the exception of the [Slovene Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral") and the [Julian Alps](/wiki/Julian_Alps "Julian Alps"). [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana"), the capital and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Slovenia "List of cities and towns in Slovenia") of Slovenia, is geographically situated near the centre of the country.[[23]](#cite_note-Mestna_občina_Ljubljana_2017-26) Other larger urban centers include [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor"), [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj"), [Kranj](/wiki/Kranj "Kranj"), [Celje](/wiki/Celje "Celje"), and [Koper](/wiki/Koper "Koper").

Slovenia's territory has been part of many different states, including the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), the [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire"), the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"), the [Republic of Venice](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice"), the [Habsburg Empire](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), [Napoleonic France](/wiki/Illyrian_Provinces "Illyrian Provinces"), the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") and the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary").[[17]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica_2021-20) The [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbs,_Croats,_and_Slovenes "Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes") was formed in December 1918; in 1929, the Kingdom became known as [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"). In 1946, after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Slovenia was established as one of six republics that made up the [Socialist Federation of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federation_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federation of Yugoslavia").[[24]](#cite_note-27) Slovenia was formed in June 1991,[[2]](#cite_note-Škrk1999-3) when the country [declared independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Slovenia "Independence of Slovenia") from Yugoslavia and became an independent [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state").[[2]](#cite_note-Škrk1999-3)

Slovenia is a [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country"), with a [high-income economy](/wiki/World_Bank_high-income_economy "World Bank high-income economy") characterized by a mixture of both traditional industries, such as manufacturing and agriculture, and modern sectors, such as information technology and financial services. The economy is highly dependent on foreign trade, with exports accounting for a significant portion of the country's GDP. Slovenia is a member of the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe"), and other associations in the global community.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name *Slovenia* etymologically means 'land of the [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs")'. The origin of the name *[Slav](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Slav#English "wikt:Slav")* itself remains uncertain. The suffix *-en* forms a [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym").[[25]](#cite_note-28)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Slovenia](/wiki/History_of_Slovenia "History of Slovenia")

### Prehistory to Slavic settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistory to Slavic settlement")]

#### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

[![A pierced cave bear bone, possibly a flute, from Divje Babe](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Divje_Babe_flute_%28Late_Pleistocene_flute%29.jpg/250px-Divje_Babe_flute_%28Late_Pleistocene_flute%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Divje_Babe_flute_(Late_Pleistocene_flute).jpg)

A [pierced cave bear bone](/wiki/Divje_Babe_flute "Divje Babe flute"), possibly a flute made by [Neanderthals](/wiki/Neanderthal "Neanderthal") dating to the [Late Pleistocene](/wiki/Late_Pleistocene "Late Pleistocene")

[![Ljubljana Marshes Wheel is the oldest wooden wheel yet discovered (Neolithic period)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Ljubljana_Marshes_Wheel_with_axle_%28oldest_wooden_wheel_yet_discovered%29.jpg/250px-Ljubljana_Marshes_Wheel_with_axle_%28oldest_wooden_wheel_yet_discovered%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ljubljana_Marshes_Wheel_with_axle_(oldest_wooden_wheel_yet_discovered).jpg)

The [Ljubljana Marshes Wheel](/wiki/Ljubljana_Marshes_Wheel "Ljubljana Marshes Wheel"), dating to the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") period, is the oldest wooden wheel yet discovered.

The area later known as Slovenia has been inhabited since [prehistoric](/wiki/Prehistoric "Prehistoric") times. There is evidence of [human habitation](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement") from around 250,000 years ago.[[26]](#cite_note-29) A [pierced cave bear bone](/wiki/Divje_Babe_flute "Divje Babe flute"), dating from 43100 ± 700 [BP](/wiki/Before_present "Before present"), found in 1995 in [Divje Babe](/wiki/Divje_Babe "Divje Babe") cave near [Cerkno](/wiki/Cerkno "Cerkno"), is considered a kind of flute, and possibly the oldest musical instrument discovered in the world.[[27]](#cite_note-30) In the 1920s and 1930s, artifacts belonging to the [Cro-Magnon](/wiki/Cro-Magnon "Cro-Magnon"), such as pierced bones, bone points, and a needle were found by archaeologist [Srečko Brodar](/wiki/Sre%C4%8Dko_Brodar "Srečko Brodar") in [Potok Cave](/wiki/Potok_Cave "Potok Cave").[[28]](#cite_note-31)[[29]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-32)

In 2002, [remains of pile dwellings](/wiki/Prehistoric_pile_dwellings_around_the_Alps "Prehistoric pile dwellings around the Alps") over 4,500 years old were discovered in the [Ljubljana Marsh](/wiki/Ljubljana_Marsh "Ljubljana Marsh"), later protected as a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site"), along with the [Ljubljana Marshes Wooden Wheel](/wiki/Ljubljana_Marshes_Wooden_Wheel "Ljubljana Marshes Wooden Wheel"), the oldest wooden wheel in the world.[[30]](#cite_note-The_Oldest_Wooden_Wheel_in_the_World-33) It shows that wooden wheels appeared almost simultaneously in Mesopotamia and Europe.[[31]](#cite_note-Slovenia-34) In the transition period between the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") to the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"), the [Urnfield](/wiki/Urnfield "Urnfield") culture flourished. Archaeological remains dating from the [Hallstatt period](/wiki/Hallstatt_period "Hallstatt period") have been found, particularly in southeastern Slovenia, among them a number of [situlas](/wiki/Situla_(vessel) "Situla (vessel)") in [Novo Mesto](/wiki/Novo_Mesto "Novo Mesto"), the "[Town of Situlas](/wiki/Situla "Situla")".[[32]](#cite_note-Situla1-35)

#### Roman era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Roman era")]

In [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") times, the area that would later become Slovenia was shared between *Venetia et Histria* (region X of [Roman Italia](/wiki/Italia_(Roman_Empire) "Italia (Roman Empire)") in the classification of [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus")) and the provinces [Pannonia](/wiki/Pannonia "Pannonia") and [Noricum](/wiki/Noricum "Noricum"). The Romans established posts at [Emona](/wiki/Emona "Emona") (Ljubljana), [Poetovio](/wiki/Poetovio "Poetovio") (Ptuj), and [Celeia](/wiki/Celeia "Celeia") (Celje); and constructed trade and military roads that ran across Slovene territory from [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") to Pannonia. In the 5th and 6th centuries, the area was subject to invasions by the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns") and [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") tribes during their incursions into Italy. Part of the inner state was protected with a defensive line of towers and walls called *[Claustra Alpium Iuliarum](/wiki/Claustra_Alpium_Iuliarum "Claustra Alpium Iuliarum")*. A [crucial battle](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Frigidus "Battle of the Frigidus") between [Theodosius I](/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I") and [Eugenius](/wiki/Eugenius "Eugenius") took place in the [Vipava Valley](/wiki/Vipava_Valley "Vipava Valley") in 394.[[33]](#cite_note-36)[[34]](#cite_note-37)

#### Slavic settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Slavic settlement")]

The [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples") tribes migrated to the Alpine area after the westward departure of the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") (the last Germanic tribe) in 568, and, under pressure from [Avars](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars"), established a [Slavic settlement in the Eastern Alps](/wiki/Slavic_settlement_of_the_Eastern_Alps "Slavic settlement of the Eastern Alps"). From 623 to 624 or possibly 626 onwards, [King Samo](/wiki/Samo "Samo") united the Alpine and Western Slavs against the Avars and Germanic peoples and established what is referred to as Samo's Kingdom. After its disintegration following Samo's death in 658 or 659, the ancestors of the [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") formed the independent [duchy of Carantania](/wiki/Carantania "Carantania"),[[35]](#cite_note-38) and [Carniola](/wiki/Carniola "Carniola"), later duchy Carniola. Other areas that would later become part of Slovenia were again ruled by Avars before [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne")'s victory over them in 803.

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Kaernten_herzogeinsetzung.jpg/250px-Kaernten_herzogeinsetzung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaernten_herzogeinsetzung.jpg)

A depiction of an ancient democratic ritual of Slovene-speaking tribes, which took place on the [Prince's Stone](/wiki/Prince%27s_Stone "Prince's Stone") in [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language") until 1414

The [Carantanians](/wiki/Carantanians "Carantanians"), one of the ancestral groups of the modern Slovenes, particularly the [Carinthian Slovenes](/wiki/Carinthian_Slovenes "Carinthian Slovenes"), were the first Slavic people to [accept Christianity](/wiki/Christianization_of_the_Slavs "Christianization of the Slavs"). They were mostly Christianized by Irish missionaries, among them [Modestus](/wiki/Modestus_(Apostle_of_Carantania) "Modestus (Apostle of Carantania)"), known as the "Apostle of Carantanians". This process, together with the Christianization of the [Bavarians](/wiki/Bavarians "Bavarians"), was later described in the memorandum known as the [Conversio Bagoariorum et Carantanorum](/wiki/Conversio_Bagoariorum_et_Carantanorum "Conversio Bagoariorum et Carantanorum"), which is thought[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] to have overemphasized the role of the Church of Salzburg in the Christianization process over similar efforts of the Patriarchate of [Aquileia](/wiki/Aquileia "Aquileia").

In the mid-8th century, Carantania became a [vassal](/wiki/Vassal "Vassal") [duchy](/wiki/Duchy "Duchy") under the rule of the [Bavarians](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), who began [spreading Christianity](/wiki/Christianization "Christianization"). Three decades later, the [Carantanians](/wiki/Carantanians "Carantanians") were incorporated, together with the Bavarians, into the [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire"). During the same period [Carniola](/wiki/Carniola "Carniola"), too, came under the Franks, and was Christianised from [Aquileia](/wiki/Aquileia "Aquileia"). Following the anti-Frankish rebellion of [Liudewit](/wiki/Liudewit "Liudewit") at the beginning of the 9th century, the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") removed the Carantanian princes, replacing them with their own border dukes. Consequently, the Frankish [feudal system](/wiki/Feudal_system "Feudal system") reached the Slovene territory.

After the victory of Emperor [Otto I](/wiki/Otto_I "Otto I") over the [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars") in 955, Slovene territory was divided into a number of border regions of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). Carantania was elevated into the [Duchy of Carinthia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Carinthia "Duchy of Carinthia") in 976.

By the 11th century, the Germanization of the area that would later become [Lower Austria](/wiki/Lower_Austria "Lower Austria"), effectively isolated the Slovene-inhabited territory from the other [western Slavs](/wiki/Western_Slavs "Western Slavs"), speeding up the development of the [Slavs of Carantania](/wiki/Carantanians "Carantanians") and of [Carniola](/wiki/Carniola "Carniola") into an independent Carantanian/Carniolans/Slovene ethnic group. By the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages"), the historic provinces of Carniola, [Styria](/wiki/Styria_(duchy) "Styria (duchy)"), [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(duchy) "Carinthia (duchy)"), [Gorizia](/wiki/County_of_Gorizia "County of Gorizia"), [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste"), and [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria") developed from the border regions and were incorporated into the medieval [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). The consolidation and formation of these historical lands took place in a long period between the 11th and 14th centuries, and were led by a number of important feudal families, such as the [Dukes of Spanheim](/wiki/Bernhard_von_Spanheim "Bernhard von Spanheim"), the [Counts of Gorizia](/wiki/Meinhardiner "Meinhardiner"), the [Counts of Celje](/wiki/Counts_of_Celje "Counts of Celje"), and, finally, the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg"). In a parallel process, an intensive Germanization significantly diminished the extent of Slovene-speaking areas. By the 15th century, the [Slovene ethnic territory](/wiki/Slovene_Lands "Slovene Lands") was reduced.[[36]](#cite_note-culture.si-39)

In 1335, [Henry of Gorizia](/wiki/Henry_of_Bohemia "Henry of Bohemia"), Duke of Carinthia, Landgrave of Carniola and Count of Tyrol, died without a male heir. His daughter [Margaret](/wiki/Margaret,_Countess_of_Tyrol "Margaret, Countess of Tyrol") was able to keep the [County of Tyrol](/wiki/County_of_Tyrol "County of Tyrol"), while the Wittelsbach emperor [Louis IV](/wiki/Louis_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Louis IV, Holy Roman Emperor") passed Carinthia and the Carniolan march to the Habsburg duke [Albert II of Austria](/wiki/Albert_II,_Duke_of_Austria "Albert II, Duke of Austria"), whose mother, [Elisabeth of Carinthia](/wiki/Elisabeth_of_Carinthia,_Queen_of_the_Romans "Elisabeth of Carinthia, Queen of the Romans"), was a sister of the late duke [Henry of Gorizia](/wiki/Henry_of_Bohemia "Henry of Bohemia"). Therefore, most of the territory that would later become Slovenia became a hereditary land of the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"). As with the other component parts of the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), Carinthia and Carniola remained a semi-autonomous state with its own constitutional structure for a long time. The [counts of Celje](/wiki/Counts_of_Celje "Counts of Celje"), a feudal family from this area who in 1436 acquired the title of state princes, were powerful competitors of the house of [Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg "Habsburg") for some time. This large dynasty, important at a European political level, had its seat in Slovene territory but died out in 1456. Its numerous large estates subsequently became the property of the Habsburgs, who retained control of the area right up until the beginning of the 20th century. [Patria del Friuli](/wiki/Patria_del_Friuli "Patria del Friuli") ruled the western portion of the lands that would later become Slovenia until [Venetian](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") takeover in 1420.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Boj_s_Turki-Valvasor.jpg/250px-Boj_s_Turki-Valvasor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boj_s_Turki-Valvasor.jpg)

The [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") army [battling](/wiki/Ottoman%E2%80%93Habsburg_wars "Ottoman–Habsburg wars") the [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") during the [Great Turkish War](/wiki/Great_Turkish_War "Great Turkish War") in the area that would later become Slovenia.

At the end of the Middle Ages, the [Slovene Lands](/wiki/Slovene_Lands "Slovene Lands") suffered a serious economic and demographic setback because of the [Turkish raids](/wiki/Ottoman%E2%80%93Habsburg_wars "Ottoman–Habsburg wars"). In 1515, a [peasant revolt](/wiki/Slovene_peasant_revolt_of_1515 "Slovene peasant revolt of 1515") spread across nearly the whole Slovene territory. In 1572 and 1573 the [Croatian-Slovenian peasant revolt](/wiki/Croatian-Slovenian_peasant_revolt "Croatian-Slovenian peasant revolt") wrought havoc throughout the wider region. Such uprisings, which often met with bloody defeats, continued throughout the 17th century.[[36]](#cite_note-culture.si-39)

### Early modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Early modern period")]

After the [dissolution of the Republic of Venice in 1797](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Republic_of_Venice "Fall of the Republic of Venice"), the Venetian Slovenia was passed to the Austrian Empire. The [Slovene Lands](/wiki/Slovene_Lands "Slovene Lands") were successively part of the French-administered [Illyrian Provinces](/wiki/Illyrian_Provinces "Illyrian Provinces") established by [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"), and [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). Slovenes inhabited most of [Carniola](/wiki/Carniola "Carniola"), the southern part of the duchies of [Carinthia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Carinthia "Duchy of Carinthia") and [Styria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Styria "Duchy of Styria"), the northern and eastern areas of the [Austrian Littoral](/wiki/Austrian_Littoral "Austrian Littoral"), as well as [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje") in the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Lands_of_the_Crown_of_Saint_Stephen "Lands of the Crown of Saint Stephen").[[37]](#cite_note-40) Industrialization was accompanied by construction of railroads to link cities and markets, but the urbanization was limited.

Due to limited opportunities, between 1880 and 1910 there was extensive emigration; around 300,000 Slovenes (1 in 6) emigrated to other countries,[[38]](#cite_note-Benderly9-41) mostly [to the US](/wiki/Slovene_Americans "Slovene Americans"), but also [to South America](/wiki/Slovene_communities_in_South_America "Slovene communities in South America") (the main part to [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina")), Germany, [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), and to larger cities in Austria-Hungary, especially [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") and [Graz](/wiki/Graz "Graz").[[38]](#cite_note-Benderly9-41) Literacy was exceptionally high, at 80–90%.[[38]](#cite_note-Benderly9-41)

The 19th century also saw a revival of culture in Slovene, accompanied by a [Romantic nationalist](/wiki/Romantic_nationalism "Romantic nationalism") quest for cultural and political autonomy. The idea of a [United Slovenia](/wiki/United_Slovenia "United Slovenia"), first advanced during the [revolutions of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848"), became the common platform of most Slovene parties and political movements in Austria-Hungary. During the same period, [Yugoslavism](/wiki/Yugoslavism "Yugoslavism"), an ideology stressing the unity of all [South Slavic peoples](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs"), spread as a reaction to [Pan-German nationalism](/wiki/Pan-Germanism "Pan-Germanism") and [Italian irredentism](/wiki/Italian_irredentism "Italian irredentism").

#### World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: World War I")]

See also: [Italian front (World War I)](/wiki/Italian_front_(World_War_I) "Italian front (World War I)"), [Battles of the Isonzo](/wiki/Battles_of_the_Isonzo "Battles of the Isonzo"), [Treaty of London (1915)](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1915) "Treaty of London (1915)"), and [Corfu Declaration](/wiki/Corfu_Declaration "Corfu Declaration")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Eingebaute_Mannschaftsunterst%C3%A4nde_am_Monte_Sabotino.jpg/250px-Eingebaute_Mannschaftsunterst%C3%A4nde_am_Monte_Sabotino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eingebaute_Mannschaftsunterst%C3%A4nde_am_Monte_Sabotino.jpg)

The [Battles of the Isonzo](/wiki/Battles_of_the_Isonzo "Battles of the Isonzo") took place mostly in rugged mountainous areas above the Soča River.

[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") brought heavy casualties to Slovenes, particularly the twelve [Battles of the Isonzo](/wiki/Battles_of_the_Isonzo "Battles of the Isonzo"). Hundreds of thousands of Slovene conscripts were drafted into the [Austro-Hungarian Army](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Army "Austro-Hungarian Army"), and over 30,000 of them died. Hundreds of thousands of Slovenes from [Princely County of Gorizia and Gradisca](/wiki/Princely_County_of_Gorizia_and_Gradisca "Princely County of Gorizia and Gradisca") were resettled in [refugee camps](/wiki/Refugee_camp "Refugee camp") in Italy and Austria. While the refugees in Austria received decent treatment, the Slovene refugees in Italian camps were treated as state enemies, and several thousand died of malnutrition and diseases between 1915 and 1918.[[39]](#cite_note-42) Entire areas of the [Slovene Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral") were destroyed.

The [Treaty of Rapallo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rapallo_(1920) "Treaty of Rapallo (1920)") of 1920 left approximately 327,000 out of the total population of 1.3 million Slovenes in Italy.[[40]](#cite_note-SacroEgoismo2012-43)[[41]](#cite_note-Cresciani_ClashOfCivilisations-44) After the [fascists took power](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome") in Italy, they were subjected to a policy of violent Fascist [Italianization](/wiki/Italianization "Italianization"). This caused the mass emigration of Slovenes, especially the middle class, from the Slovene Littoral and [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") to [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia") and South America. Those who remained organized several connected networks of both passive and armed resistance. The best known was the [militant anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism") organization [TIGR](/wiki/TIGR "TIGR"), formed in 1927 to fight Fascist oppression of the Slovene and Croat populations in the [Julian March](/wiki/Julian_March "Julian March").[[42]](#cite_note-Mira_Cencič_1997-45)[[43]](#cite_note-Tatjana_Rejec_1995-46)

### State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/KongressfallofAH.jpg/250px-KongressfallofAH.jpg)](/wiki/File:KongressfallofAH.jpg)

The proclamation of the State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs at [Congress Square](/wiki/Congress_Square "Congress Square") in Ljubljana on 29 October 1918

The [Slovene People's Party](/wiki/Slovene_People%27s_Party_(historical) "Slovene People's Party (historical)") launched a movement for self-determination, demanding the creation of a semi-independent [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavic_peoples "South Slavic peoples") state under [Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg_dynasty "Habsburg dynasty") rule. The proposal was picked up by most Slovene parties, and a mass mobilization of Slovene civil society, known as the [Declaration Movement](/w/index.php?title=May_Declaration_(1917)&action=edit&redlink=1 "May Declaration (1917) (page does not exist)"), followed.[[44]](#cite_note-47) This demand was rejected by the Austrian political elites; but following the dissolution of the Austro-Hungarian Empire in the aftermath of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), the [National Council of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs](/wiki/National_Council_of_Slovenes,_Croats_and_Serbs "National Council of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs") took power in [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb") on 6 October 1918. On 29 October, independence was declared by a national gathering in Ljubljana, and by the Croatian parliament, declaring the establishment of the new [State of Slovenes, Croats, and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats,_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats, and Serbs").

### Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes (later the Kingdom of Yugoslavia)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes (later the Kingdom of Yugoslavia)")]

See also: [Creation of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Creation_of_Yugoslavia "Creation of Yugoslavia"), [Carinthian Plebiscite](/wiki/Carinthian_Plebiscite "Carinthian Plebiscite"), and [Rudolf Maister](/wiki/Rudolf_Maister "Rudolf Maister")

On 1 December 1918, the State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs merged with [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"), becoming part of the new [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbs,_Croats,_and_Slovenes "Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes"); in 1929, it was renamed the [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"). The area that would later become Slovenia, being the most industrialized and westernized compared to other less developed parts of Yugoslavia, became the main centre of industrial production. Compared to Serbia, Slovene industrial production was four times greater; and it was 22 times greater than in [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia"). The interwar period brought further industrialization in the area that would become Slovenia, with rapid economic growth in the 1920s, followed by a relatively successful economic adjustment to the [1929 economic crisis](/wiki/1929_economic_crisis "1929 economic crisis") and [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").

Although Slovenia did not exist as an autonomous administrative unit between 1921 and 1941, the [Drava Banovina](/wiki/Drava_Banovina "Drava Banovina") of the [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") was frequently called simply "Slovenia", even in some official documents.[[45]](#cite_note-48)[[46]](#cite_note-49)[[47]](#cite_note-50)

Following a [plebiscite](/wiki/Carinthian_Plebiscite "Carinthian Plebiscite") in October 1920, the Slovene-speaking southern [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_(state) "Carinthia (state)") was ceded to [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"). With the [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon"), the Kingdom of Yugoslavia was awarded the mostly Slovene-inhabited [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje") region, formerly part of Austria-Hungary. Slovenes living in territories that fell under the rule of the neighboring states—Italy, Austria, and Hungary—were subjected to [assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation").

### World War II and annexation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: World War II and annexation")]

Main article: [World War II in the Slovene Lands](/wiki/World_War_II_in_the_Slovene_Lands "World War II in the Slovene Lands")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Provincia_di_Lubiana1941-1943.jpg/500px-Provincia_di_Lubiana1941-1943.jpg)](/wiki/File:Provincia_di_Lubiana1941-1943.jpg)

[Nazi Germany](/wiki/Austria_under_National_Socialism "Austria under National Socialism")

[Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%9346) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–46)")

[Fascist  
  
Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy")

[Independent  
  
State of  
  
Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia")

During World War II, [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–1946)") annexed northern areas (brown and dark green areas, respectively), while Fascist [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") annexed the vertically hatched black area (solid black western part having been annexed by Italy in 1920 with the [Treaty of Rapallo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rapallo_(1920) "Treaty of Rapallo (1920)")). Some villages were incorporated into the [Independent State of Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia"). After 1943, Germany also occupied the area that Italy had annexed.

The area later known as Slovenia was trisected and completely annexed into [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and Fascist [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") during World War II.[[48]](#cite_note-GJK_2013-51) In addition, the [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje") region in the east was annexed to Hungary, and some villages in the [Lower Sava Valley](/wiki/Lower_Sava_Valley "Lower Sava Valley") were incorporated in the newly created Nazi puppet [Independent State of Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia") (NDH). Axis forces [invaded Yugoslavia](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia") in April 1941 and defeated the country in a few weeks. The southern part, including [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana"), was annexed to Italy, while the Nazis took over the northern and eastern parts of the country. The Nazis had a plan of [ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing") of these areas,[[49]](#cite_note-HF2006_BerghahnBooks-52) and they resettled or expelled the local Slovene civilian population to the puppet states of [Nedić's Serbia](/wiki/Nedi%C4%87%27s_Serbia "Nedić's Serbia") (7,500) and [NDH](/wiki/NDH "NDH") (10,000). Furthermore the annexed government exiled over 10 000 jews into German concentration camps. In addition, some 46,000 Slovenes were expelled to Germany, including children who were separated from their parents and allocated to German families.[[50]](#cite_note-LM2009_UNC-53)[[51]](#cite_note-54) At the same time, the ethnic Germans in the [Gottschee](/wiki/Gottschee "Gottschee") enclave in the Italian annexation zone were resettled to the Nazi-controlled areas cleansed of their Slovene population.[[52]](#cite_note-L1993_UNC-55)
Around 30,000 to 40,000 Slovene men were drafted to the [German Army](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") and sent to the Eastern front. Slovene was banned from education, and its use in public life was limited.[[48]](#cite_note-GJK_2013-51)

The south-central portion of the area that would later become Slovenia was annexed by Fascist Italy and renamed the [Province of Ljubljana](/wiki/Province_of_Ljubljana "Province of Ljubljana"). The [Slovenian National Liberation Front](/wiki/Liberation_Front_of_the_Slovene_Nation "Liberation Front of the Slovene Nation") was organized there in April 1941. Led by the Communist Party, it formed the [Slovene Partisan](/wiki/Slovene_Partisans "Slovene Partisans") units as part of the [Yugoslav Partisans](/wiki/Yugoslav_Partisans "Yugoslav Partisans") led by the Communist leader [Josip Broz Tito](/wiki/Josip_Broz_Tito "Josip Broz Tito").[[53]](#cite_note-JJR2013_OxfordPress-56)[[54]](#cite_note-AS_2005-57)

After the resistance started in summer 1941, Italian violence against the Slovene civilian population escalated. The Italian authorities deported some 25,000 people to [concentration camps](/wiki/Italian_concentration_camps "Italian concentration camps"), which equaled 7.5% of the population of their occupation zone. The most infamous ones were [Rab](/wiki/Rab_concentration_camp "Rab concentration camp") and [Gonars](/wiki/Gonars_concentration_camp "Gonars concentration camp"). To counter the Communist-led insurgence, the Italians sponsored local anti-guerrilla units, formed mostly by the local conservative Catholic Slovene population that resented the revolutionary violence of the partisans. After the [Italian armistice](/wiki/Italian_armistice "Italian armistice") of September 1943, the Germans took over both the Province of Ljubljana and the Slovenian Littoral, incorporating them into what was known as the [Operation Zone of Adriatic Coastal Region](/wiki/Operation_Zone_of_Adriatic_Coastal_Region "Operation Zone of Adriatic Coastal Region"). They united the Slovene anti-Communist counter-insurgence into the [Slovene Home Guard](/wiki/Slovene_Home_Guard "Slovene Home Guard") and appointed a puppet regime in the Province of Ljubljana. The anti-Nazi resistance however expanded, creating its own administrative structures as the basis for Slovene statehood within a new, federal and socialist Yugoslavia.[[55]](#cite_note-Tomasevich_2001-58)[[56]](#cite_note-Dennison_I._Rusinow_1978,_p._2-59)

In 1945, [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia") was liberated by the partisan resistance and soon became a socialist federation known as the [People's Federal Republic of Yugoslavia](/wiki/People%27s_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "People's Federal Republic of Yugoslavia").

Approximately 8% of the Slovene population died during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The small Jewish community, mostly in the [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje") region, was destroyed in 1944 in the [holocaust of Hungarian Jews](/wiki/Holocaust_in_Hungary "Holocaust in Hungary"). The German-speaking minority, amounting to 2.5% of the Slovenian population prior to the war, was either expelled or killed in the aftermath of the war. Hundreds of [Istrian Italians](/wiki/Istrian_Italians "Istrian Italians") and Slovenes that were members of fascist and collaborationist forces, alongside civilians presumed to oppose communism, were killed in the [foibe massacres](/wiki/Foibe_killings "Foibe killings"), and more than 25,000 fled or were expelled from [Slovenian Istria](/wiki/Slovenian_Istria "Slovenian Istria").[[57]](#cite_note-60)[[58]](#cite_note-61) Around 130,000 persons, mostly political and military opponents, were executed in May and June 1945.[[59]](#cite_note-62)

### Socialist Republic of Slovenia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Socialist Republic of Slovenia")]

During the re-establishment of Yugoslavia in World War II, the [Socialist Republic of Slovenia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Slovenia "Socialist Republic of Slovenia") was created and became part of [Federal Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"). Yugoslavia was a [socialist state](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state"), but because of the [Tito–Stalin split](/wiki/Tito%E2%80%93Stalin_split "Tito–Stalin split") in 1948, economic and personal freedoms were much broader than in the [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") countries. In 1947, the [Slovene Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral") and the western half of [Inner Carniola](/wiki/Inner_Carniola "Inner Carniola"), which had been annexed by Italy after World War One, were annexed to Slovenia.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Economy_Yugoslavia_1975.png/250px-Economy_Yugoslavia_1975.png)](/wiki/File:Economy_Yugoslavia_1975.png)

Average strength of Yugoslav economy as a deviation from the main (Yugoslavia = 100 %) indicator 1975. [SR Slovenia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Slovenia "Socialist Republic of Slovenia") (dark green) was, along with [SR Croatia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Croatia "Socialist Republic of Croatia") and [SAP Vojvodina](/wiki/Socialist_Autonomous_Province_of_Vojvodina "Socialist Autonomous Province of Vojvodina") (light green), the richest entity of [SFR Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia").

After the failure of [forced collectivisation](/wiki/Collective_farming "Collective farming") that was attempted from 1949 to 1953, a policy of gradual economic liberalisation, known as [workers self-management](/wiki/Workers%27_self-management "Workers' self-management"), was introduced under the advice and supervision of the Slovene Marxist theoretician and Communist leader [Edvard Kardelj](/wiki/Edvard_Kardelj "Edvard Kardelj"), the main ideologue of the [Titoist](/wiki/Titoism "Titoism") path to socialism. Suspected opponents of this policy both from within and outside the Communist party were persecuted and thousands were sent to [Goli otok](/wiki/Goli_otok "Goli otok") (where also opponents from [Tito-Stalin split](/wiki/Tito%E2%80%93Stalin_split "Tito–Stalin split"), which developed at the same time, were sent).

The late 1950s saw a policy of liberalization in the cultural sphere as well, and unlimited border crossing into western countries was allowed, both for Yugoslav citizens and for foreigners. In 1956, [Josip Broz Tito](/wiki/Josip_Broz_Tito "Josip Broz Tito"), together with other leaders, founded the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"). In the 1950s, Slovenia's economy developed rapidly and was strongly industrialized. With further economic decentralization of Yugoslavia in 1965–66, Slovenia's [domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") was 2.5 times the average of Yugoslav republics. While a Communist country, after the Tito–Stalin split Yugoslavia initiated a period of military neutrality and non-alignment. JAT Yugoslav Airlines was the flag carrier and during its existence it grew to become one of the leading airlines in Europe both by fleet and destinations. By the 1970s more airlines were created including Slovenian [Adria Airways](/wiki/Adria_Airways "Adria Airways") mostly focused in the growing tourist industry. Until the 1980s, [Slovenia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Slovenia "Socialist Republic of Slovenia") enjoyed relatively broad autonomy within the federation. It was the most liberal communist state in Europe, and the passport of the Yugoslavia Federation allowed Yugoslavs to travel to the most world countries of any socialist country during the Cold War. Many people worked in western countries, which reduced unemployment in their home country.

Opposition to the regime was mostly limited to intellectual and literary circles and became especially vocal after Tito's death in 1980 when the economic and political situation in Yugoslavia became very strained.[[36]](#cite_note-culture.si-39) Political disputes around economic measures were echoed in the public sentiment, as many Slovenians felt they were being economically exploited, having to sustain an expensive and inefficient federal administration.

### Slovenian Spring, democracy and independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Slovenian Spring, democracy and independence")]

In 1987 a group of intellectuals demanded Slovene independence in the [57th edition](/wiki/Contributions_for_the_Slovenian_National_Program "Contributions for the Slovenian National Program") of the magazine *[Nova revija](/wiki/Nova_revija_(magazine) "Nova revija (magazine)")*. Demands for democratisation and more Slovenian independence were sparked off. A mass democratic movement, coordinated by the [Committee for the Defence of Human Rights](/wiki/Committee_for_the_Defence_of_Human_Rights "Committee for the Defence of Human Rights"), pushed the Communists in the direction of democratic reforms.

In September 1989, numerous [constitutional amendments](/wiki/Constitutional_amendment "Constitutional amendment") were passed to introduce [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") to Slovenia.[[60]](#cite_note-63)[[61]](#cite_note-64) On 7 March 1990, the Slovenian Assembly changed the official name of the state to the "Republic of Slovenia".[[62]](#cite_note-twenty.si-65)[[63]](#cite_note-66) In April 1990, the first democratic election in Slovenia took place, and the united opposition movement [DEMOS](/wiki/Democratic_Opposition_of_Slovenia "Democratic Opposition of Slovenia") led by [Jože Pučnik](/wiki/Jo%C5%BEe_Pu%C4%8Dnik "Jože Pučnik") emerged victorious.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Teritorialci_so_z_armbrustom_zadeli_tank_v_kri%C5%BEi%C5%A1%C4%8Du_pred_MMP_Ro%C5%BEna_Dolina..jpg/250px-Teritorialci_so_z_armbrustom_zadeli_tank_v_kri%C5%BEi%C5%A1%C4%8Du_pred_MMP_Ro%C5%BEna_Dolina..jpg)](/wiki/File:Teritorialci_so_z_armbrustom_zadeli_tank_v_kri%C5%BEi%C5%A1%C4%8Du_pred_MMP_Ro%C5%BEna_Dolina..jpg)

Slovenian Territorial Defense Units counterattacking a [Yugoslav People's Army](/wiki/Yugoslav_People%27s_Army "Yugoslav People's Army") tank which entered Slovenia during the [Ten-Day War](/wiki/Ten-Day_War "Ten-Day War"), 1991

The initial revolutionary events in Slovenia pre-dated the [Revolutions of 1989](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989") in Eastern Europe by almost a year, but went largely unnoticed by international observers. On 23 December 1990, more than 88% of the electorate voted for a sovereign and independent Slovenia.[[64]](#cite_note-Felicijan-67)[[65]](#cite_note-STAT11-68) On 25 June 1991, Slovenia became independent.[[2]](#cite_note-Škrk1999-3)[[66]](#cite_note-Jonsson-69) On 27 June in the early morning, the [Yugoslav People's Army](/wiki/Yugoslav_People%27s_Army "Yugoslav People's Army") dispatched its forces to prevent further measures for the establishment of a new country, which led to the [Ten-Day War](/wiki/Ten-Day_War "Ten-Day War").[[67]](#cite_note-Race-70)[[68]](#cite_note-SAF_History-71) On 7 July, the [Brijuni Agreement](/wiki/Brijuni_Agreement "Brijuni Agreement") was signed, implementing a truce and a three-month halt of the enforcement of Slovenia's independence.[[69]](#cite_note-72) At the end of the month, the last soldiers of the Yugoslav Army left Slovenia.

In December 1991, a new [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Slovenia "Constitution of Slovenia") was adopted,[[66]](#cite_note-Jonsson-69) followed in 1992 by the laws on [denationalisation and privatisation](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization").[[70]](#cite_note-Klemenčič2004-73) The members of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") recognised Slovenia as an independent state on 15 January 1992, and the United Nations accepted it as a member on 22 May 1992.[[71]](#cite_note-Borak2004-74)

Slovenia joined the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") on 1 May 2004.[[72]](#cite_note-75) Slovenia has one Commissioner in the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), and seven Slovene parliamentarians were elected to the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") at elections on 13 June 2004. In 2004 Slovenia also joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO").[[73]](#cite_note-76) Slovenia subsequently succeeded in meeting the [Maastricht criteria](/wiki/Maastricht_criteria "Maastricht criteria") and joined the [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") (the first transition country to do so) on 1 January 2007.[[74]](#cite_note-77) It was the first post-Communist country to hold the [Presidency of the Council of the European Union](/wiki/Presidency_of_the_Council_of_the_European_Union "Presidency of the Council of the European Union"), for the first six months of 2008. On 21 July 2010, it became a member of the OECD.[[75]](#cite_note-78)

The disillusionment with domestic socio-economic elites at municipal and national levels was expressed at the [2012–2013 Slovenian protests](/wiki/2012%E2%80%932013_Slovenian_protests "2012–2013 Slovenian protests") on a wider scale than in the smaller [15 October 2011 protests](/wiki/15_October_2011_global_protests#Slovenia "15 October 2011 global protests").[[76]](#cite_note-79) In relation to the leading politicians' response to allegations made by the official [Commission for the Prevention of Corruption of the Republic of Slovenia](/wiki/Commission_for_the_Prevention_of_Corruption_of_the_Republic_of_Slovenia "Commission for the Prevention of Corruption of the Republic of Slovenia"), legal experts expressed the need for changes in the system that would limit political [arbitrariness](/wiki/Arbitrariness "Arbitrariness").[[77]](#cite_note-80)[*[needs context](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Provide_context_for_the_reader "Wikipedia:Writing better articles")*]

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Slovenia](/wiki/Geography_of_Slovenia "Geography of Slovenia")

See also: [Protected areas of Slovenia](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Slovenia "Protected areas of Slovenia")

Slovenia is located in [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") touching the eastern [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") and bordering the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea").[[78]](#cite_note-81)[[79]](#cite_note-82)[[80]](#cite_note-83) This locates Slovenia within the [Mediterranean basin](/wiki/Mediterranean_basin "Mediterranean basin").[[81]](#cite_note-84) It lies between latitudes [45°](/wiki/45th_parallel_north "45th parallel north") and [47° N](/wiki/47th_parallel_north "47th parallel north"), and longitudes [13°](/wiki/13th_meridian_east "13th meridian east") and [17° E](/wiki/17th_meridian_east "17th meridian east"). The [15th meridian east](/wiki/15th_meridian_east "15th meridian east") almost corresponds to the middle line of the country in the direction west–east.[[82]](#cite_note-85) The [Geometric Centre of the Republic of Slovenia](/wiki/Geometric_Centre_of_the_Republic_of_Slovenia "Geometric Centre of the Republic of Slovenia") is located at [coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") 46°07'11.8" N and 14°48'55.2" E.[[83]](#cite_note-86) It lies in [Slivna](/wiki/Slivna "Slivna") in the Municipality of [Litija](/wiki/Litija "Litija").[[84]](#cite_note-87) Slovenia's highest peak, [Triglav](/wiki/Triglav "Triglav") (2,864 m or 9,396 ft), has become a national symbol of Slovenia, featured on the national [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Slovenia "Coat of arms of Slovenia") and [flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Slovenia "Flag of Slovenia"). The country's average height [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level") is 557 m (1,827 ft).

Four major European geographic regions meet in Slovenia: the Alps, the [Dinarides](/wiki/Dinaric_Alps "Dinaric Alps"), the [Pannonian Plain](/wiki/Pannonian_Plain "Pannonian Plain"), and the Mediterranean Sea. Although on the shore of the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") near the Mediterranean Sea, most of Slovenia is in the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") [drainage basin](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin"). The Alps—including the [Julian Alps](/wiki/Julian_Alps "Julian Alps"), the [Kamnik-Savinja Alps](/wiki/Kamnik-Savinja_Alps "Kamnik-Savinja Alps") and the [Karawank](/wiki/Karawanks "Karawanks") chain, as well as the [Pohorje](/wiki/Pohorje "Pohorje") massif—dominate Northern Slovenia along its long border with [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"). Slovenia's Adriatic coastline stretches approximately 47 kilometres (29 mi)[[85]](#cite_note-88) from Italy to Croatia.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Mangart_16.jpg/250px-Mangart_16.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mangart_16.jpg)

Mount [Mangart](/wiki/Mangart "Mangart"), in the [Julian Alps](/wiki/Julian_Alps "Julian Alps"), is the third-highest peak in Slovenia, after [Triglav](/wiki/Triglav "Triglav") and [Škrlatica](/wiki/%C5%A0krlatica "Škrlatica").

The term "[Karst topography](/wiki/Karst_topography "Karst topography")" refers to that of southwestern Slovenia's [Karst Plateau](/wiki/Karst_Plateau "Karst Plateau"), a [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") region of underground rivers, gorges, and caves, between Ljubljana and the Mediterranean Sea. On the [Pannonian plain](/wiki/Pannonian_plain "Pannonian plain") to the East and Northeast, toward the Croatian and Hungarian borders, the landscape is essentially flat. However, most of Slovenia is hilly or mountainous, with around 90% of its land surface 200 m (656 ft) or more [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").

As of 2020, 60.4% of Slovenia (12,380 km2 or 4,780 sq mi) is forested,[[86]](#cite_note-fao_gfra2020-89) ranking it second in Europe by [percentage of area forested](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_forest_area "List of countries by forest area"), after [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland").[[87]](#cite_note-owid_land_cover_forest-90) The areas are covered mostly by [beech](/wiki/Beech "Beech"), [fir](/wiki/Fir "Fir")-beech and beech-[oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") forests and have a relatively high production capacity.[[88]](#cite_note-91) Remnants of [primeval forests](/wiki/Old_growth "Old growth") are still to be found, the largest in the [Kočevje](/wiki/Ko%C4%8Devje "Kočevje") area. Grassland covers 5,593 km2 (2,159 sq mi) and fields and gardens (954 km2 or 368 sq mi). There are 363 km2 (140 sq mi) of orchards and 216 km2 (83 sq mi) of vineyards.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Geology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/%C5%BDlebi%C4%8Di.jpg/250px-%C5%BDlebi%C4%8Di.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C5%BDlebi%C4%8Di.jpg)

Solution runnels (also known as rillenkarren) are a [karst](/wiki/Karst_topography "Karst topography") feature on the [Karst Plateau](/wiki/Karst_Plateau "Karst Plateau"), as in many other karst areas of the world.

Slovenia is in a rather active [seismic zone](/wiki/Seismic_zone "Seismic zone") because of its position on the small [Adriatic Plate](/wiki/Adriatic_Plate "Adriatic Plate"), which is squeezed between the [Eurasian Plate](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate") to the north and the [African Plate](/wiki/African_Plate "African Plate") to the south and rotates counter-clockwise.[[89]](#cite_note-Seisme-92) Thus the country is at the junction of three important [geotectonic](/wiki/Tectonics "Tectonics") units: the Alps to the north, the Dinaric Alps to the south and the Pannonian Basin to the east.[[89]](#cite_note-Seisme-92) Scientists have been able to identify 60 destructive earthquakes in the past. Additionally, a network of seismic stations is active throughout the country.[[89]](#cite_note-Seisme-92)

Many parts of Slovenia have a [carbonate](/wiki/Carbonate_rock "Carbonate rock") [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") and extensive [cave systems](/wiki/Cave_system "Cave system") have developed.

### Natural regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Natural regions")]

The first regionalisations of Slovenia were made by geographers [Anton Melik](/wiki/Anton_Melik "Anton Melik") (1935–1936) and [Svetozar Ilešič](/w/index.php?title=Svetozar_Ile%C5%A1i%C4%8D&action=edit&redlink=1 "Svetozar Ilešič (page does not exist)") (1968). The newer regionalisation by [Ivan Gams](/w/index.php?title=Ivan_Gams&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ivan Gams (page does not exist)") divided Slovenia in the following macroregions:[[90]](#cite_note-93)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Strunjan_bay_%2824209577441%29.jpg/250px-Strunjan_bay_%2824209577441%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Strunjan_bay_(24209577441).jpg)

Slovenian coast with cliffs

* the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") (*Alpe*)
* the [subalpine landscapes](/wiki/Slovene_Prealps "Slovene Prealps") (*predalpski svet*)
* the [Slovene Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral") or [Submediterranean](/wiki/Submediterranean "Submediterranean") Slovenia (*Primorje* or *submediteranska Slovenija*)
* the [Dinaric](/wiki/Dinaric_Alps "Dinaric Alps") [plateaus](/wiki/Plateaus "Plateaus") of the continental Slovenia (*dinarske planote celinske Slovenije*)
* [Subpannonian Slovenia](/wiki/Pannonian_Plain "Pannonian Plain") (*subpanonska Slovenija*)

According to a newer natural geographic regionalisation, the country consists of four [macroregions](/wiki/Macroregion "Macroregion"). These are the Alpine, the Mediterranean, the Dinaric, and the [Pannonian](/wiki/Pannonia "Pannonia") landscapes. Macroregions are defined according to major relief units (the Alps, the Pannonian plain, the Dinaric mountains) and climate types (submediterranean, temperate continental, mountain climate).[[91]](#cite_note-94) These are often quite interwoven.

Protected areas of Slovenia include national parks, regional parks, and nature parks, the largest of which is [Triglav National Park](/wiki/Triglav_National_Park "Triglav National Park"). There are 286 [Natura 2000](/wiki/Natura_2000 "Natura 2000") designated protected areas, which include 36% of the country's land area, the largest percentage among European Union states.[[92]](#cite_note-95) Additionally, according to [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University")'s [Environmental Performance Index](/wiki/Environmental_Performance_Index "Environmental Performance Index"), Slovenia is considered a "strong performer" in environmental protection efforts.[[93]](#cite_note-96)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Climate_types_Slovenia_1970-2000.JPG/500px-Climate_types_Slovenia_1970-2000.JPG)](/wiki/File:Climate_types_Slovenia_1970-2000.JPG)

Climate types of Slovenia 1970–2000 and [climographs](/wiki/Climograph "Climograph") for selected settlements

Slovenia is located in temperate latitudes. The climate is also influenced by the variety of relief, and the influence of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") and the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea"). In the northeast, the [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") type with the greatest difference between winter and summer temperatures prevails. In the coastal region, there is a sub-[Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"). The effect of the sea on the temperature rates is also visible up the [Soča](/wiki/So%C4%8Da "Soča") Valley, while a severe [Alpine climate](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") is present in the high mountain regions. There is a strong interaction between these three climatic systems across most of the country.[[94]](#cite_note-arso.gov.si-97)[[95]](#cite_note-arso.gov.si_Meteo_Archive-98)

[Precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)"), often coming from the [Gulf of Genoa](/wiki/Gulf_of_Genoa "Gulf of Genoa"),[[96]](#cite_note-99) varies across the country as well, with over 3,500 mm (138 in) in some western regions and dropping down to 800 mm (31 in) in [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje"). Snow is quite frequent in winter and the record snow cover in Ljubljana was recorded in 1952 at 146 cm (57 in).

Compared to Western Europe, Slovenia is not very windy, because it lies in the slipstream of the Alps. The average wind speeds are lower than in the plains of the nearby countries. Due to the rugged terrain, local vertical winds with daily periods are present. Besides these, there are three winds of particular regional importance: the [bora](/wiki/Bora_(wind) "Bora (wind)"), the [jugo](/wiki/Jugo "Jugo"), and the [foehn](/wiki/Foehn_wind "Foehn wind"). The jugo and the bora are characteristic of the Littoral. Whereas the jugo is humid and warm, the bora is usually cold and gusty. The foehn is typical of the Alpine regions in northern Slovenia. Generally present in Slovenia are the northeast wind, the southeast wind and the [north wind](/wiki/North_wind "North wind").[[97]](#cite_note-100)

### Waters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Waters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Lake_Bohinj_%289452094709%29.jpg/250px-Lake_Bohinj_%289452094709%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Bohinj_(9452094709).jpg)

[Lake Bohinj](/wiki/Lake_Bohinj "Lake Bohinj"), the largest Slovenian lake and one of the two headwaters of the [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava") River

81% of the territory of Slovenia (16,423 square kilometers or 6,341 square miles) belongs to the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") basin, with the remaining 19% (3,850 square kilometers or 1,490 square miles) lying within the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") basin. These watersheds are divided into sub-basins according to their central rivers, the [Mura](/wiki/Mur_(river) "Mur (river)") River basin, the [Drava](/wiki/Drava "Drava") River basin, the [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava") River basin with [Kolpa](/wiki/Kupa "Kupa") River basin, and the basin of the Adriatic rivers.[[98]](#cite_note-101) In comparison with other [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_countries "Developed countries"), water quality in Slovenia is considered to be among the highest in Europe. One of the reasons is undoubtedly that most of the rivers rise on the mountainous territory of Slovenia. However, this does not mean that Slovenia has no problems with surface water and groundwater quality, especially in areas with [intensive farming](/wiki/Intensive_farming "Intensive farming").[[99]](#cite_note-102)

### Biodiversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Biodiversity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/P_anguinus2.jpg/250px-P_anguinus2.jpg)](/wiki/File:P_anguinus2.jpg)

[Olm](/wiki/Olm "Olm") can be found in the [Postojna cave](/wiki/Postojna_cave "Postojna cave") and other caves in the country.

Slovenia signed the Rio [Convention on Biological Diversity](/wiki/Convention_on_Biological_Diversity "Convention on Biological Diversity") on 13 June 1992 and became a party to the convention on 9 July 1996.[[100]](#cite_note-103) It subsequently produced a [National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan](/wiki/Biodiversity_action_plan "Biodiversity action plan"), which was received by the convention on 30 May 2002.

Slovenia is distinguished by an exceptionally wide variety of habitats,[[101]](#cite_note-104) due to the contact of geological units and biogeographical regions, and due to human influences. The country is home to four terrestrial ecoregions: [Dinaric Mountains mixed forests](/wiki/Dinaric_Mountains_mixed_forests "Dinaric Mountains mixed forests"), [Pannonian mixed forests](/wiki/Pannonian_mixed_forests "Pannonian mixed forests"), [Alps conifer and mixed forests](/wiki/Alps_conifer_and_mixed_forests "Alps conifer and mixed forests"), and [Illyrian deciduous forests](/wiki/Illyrian_deciduous_forests "Illyrian deciduous forests").[[102]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-105) Around 12.5% of the territory is protected with 35.5% in the [Natura 2000](/wiki/Natura_2000 "Natura 2000") ecological network.[[103]](#cite_note-106) Despite this, because of pollution and environmental degradation, diversity has been in decline. Slovenia had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 3.78/10, ranking it 140th globally out of 172 countries.[[104]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-107)

#### Animals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Animals")]

The [biological diversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") of the country is high, with 1% of the world's organisms on 0.004% of the Earth's surface area.[[105]](#cite_note-108) There are 75 mammal species, among them [marmots](/wiki/Marmot "Marmot"), [Alpine ibex](/wiki/Alpine_ibex "Alpine ibex"), and [chamois](/wiki/Chamois "Chamois"). There are numerous [deer](/wiki/Deer "Deer"), [roe deer](/wiki/Roe_deer "Roe deer"), [boar](/wiki/Boar "Boar"), and [hares](/wiki/Hare "Hare").[[106]](#cite_note-NFPRes-109) The [edible dormouse](/wiki/Edible_dormouse "Edible dormouse") is often found in the Slovenian beech forests. Trapping these animals is a long tradition and is part of Slovenian national identity.[[107]](#cite_note-110)

Some important carnivores include the [Eurasian lynx](/wiki/Eurasian_lynx "Eurasian lynx"),[[108]](#cite_note-111)[[109]](#cite_note-112) [European wild cats](/wiki/European_wild_cat "European wild cat"), foxes (especially the [red fox](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox")), and [European jackal](/wiki/European_jackal "European jackal").[[110]](#cite_note-113) There are [hedgehogs](/wiki/Hedgehog "Hedgehog"), [martens](/wiki/Marten "Marten"), and snakes such as [vipers](/wiki/Viperinae "Viperinae") and [grass snakes](/wiki/Grass_snake "Grass snake"). According to recent estimates, Slovenia has c. 40–60 [wolves](/wiki/Gray_wolf "Gray wolf")[[111]](#cite_note-114) and about 450 [brown bears](/wiki/Brown_bear "Brown bear").[[112]](#cite_note-115)[[113]](#cite_note-116)

Slovenia is home to an exceptionally diverse number of cave species, with a few tens of [endemic species](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism").[[114]](#cite_note-117) Among the cave vertebrates, the only known one is the [olm](/wiki/Olm "Olm"), living in Karst, Lower Carniola, and White Carniola.

The only regular species of [cetaceans](/wiki/Cetacea "Cetacea") found in the northern Adriatic sea is the [bottlenose dolphin](/wiki/Bottlenose_dolphin "Bottlenose dolphin") (*Tursiops truncatus*).[[115]](#cite_note-118)

There are a wide variety of birds, such as the [tawny owl](/wiki/Tawny_owl "Tawny owl"), the [long-eared owl](/wiki/Long-eared_owl "Long-eared owl"), the [eagle owl](/wiki/Eagle_owl "Eagle owl"), [hawks](/wiki/Hawk "Hawk"), and [short-toed eagles](/wiki/Short-toed_eagle "Short-toed eagle"). Other birds of prey have been recorded, as well as a growing number of ravens, [crows](/wiki/Crow "Crow") and [magpies](/wiki/Magpie "Magpie") migrating into Ljubljana and Maribor where they thrive.[[116]](#cite_note-119) Other birds include [black](/wiki/Black_woodpecker "Black woodpecker") and [green](/wiki/European_green_woodpecker "European green woodpecker") [woodpeckers](/wiki/Woodpecker "Woodpecker") and the [white stork](/wiki/White_stork "White stork"), which nests mainly in [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Lipica_horses_%287198987762%29.jpg/500px-Lipica_horses_%287198987762%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lipica_horses_(7198987762).jpg)

Modern [Lipizzaner](/wiki/Lipizzaner "Lipizzaner") grazing

There are 13 domestic animals native to Slovenia,[[117]](#cite_note-120) of eight species (hen, pig, dog, horse, sheep, goat, honey bee, and cattle).[[118]](#cite_note-Meglič2003-121) Among these are the [Karst Shepherd](/wiki/Karst_Shepherd "Karst Shepherd"),[[119]](#cite_note-FCI2009doc-122) the [Carniolan honeybee](/wiki/Carniolan_honeybee "Carniolan honeybee"), and the [Lipizzan](/wiki/Lipizzan "Lipizzan") horse.[[118]](#cite_note-Meglič2003-121) The [marble trout](/wiki/Marble_trout "Marble trout") or marmorata (*Salmo marmoratus*) is an indigenous Slovenian fish.[[120]](#cite_note-Balkan_Trout_Restoration_Group-123) Extensive breeding programmes have been introduced to repopulate the marble trout into lakes and streams invaded by non-indigenous species of [trout](/wiki/Trout "Trout"). Slovenia is also home to the [wels catfish](/wiki/Wels_catfish "Wels catfish").

More than 2,400 fungal species have been recorded from Slovenia[[121]](#cite_note-124) and, since that figure does not include lichen-forming fungi, the total number of Slovenian fungi already known is undoubtedly much higher. Many more remain to be discovered.

Slovenia is the second most-forested country in Europe,[[86]](#cite_note-fao_gfra2020-89) with 60.4% of the territory covered by forests.[[87]](#cite_note-owid_land_cover_forest-90) The forests are an important natural resource, and logging is kept to a minimum.[[122]](#cite_note-125) In the interior of the country are typical Central European forests, predominantly [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") and [beech](/wiki/Beech "Beech"). In the mountains, [spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce"), [fir](/wiki/Fir "Fir"), and [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine") are more common. Pine trees grow on the [Karst Plateau](/wiki/Karst_Plateau "Karst Plateau"), although only one-third of the region is covered by pine forest. The [lime/linden](/wiki/Tilia "Tilia") tree, common in Slovenian forests, is a national symbol. The [tree line](/wiki/Tree_line "Tree line") is at 1,700 to 1,800 metres (5,600 to 5,900 feet).[[123]](#cite_note-126)

In the Alps, flowers such as *[Daphne blagayana](/wiki/Daphne_blagayana "Daphne blagayana")*, [gentians](/wiki/Gentian "Gentian") (*[Gentiana clusii](/wiki/Gentiana_clusii "Gentiana clusii")*, *[Gentiana froelichii](/wiki/Gentiana_froelichii "Gentiana froelichii")*), *[Primula auricula](/wiki/Primula_auricula "Primula auricula")*, [edelweiss](/wiki/Leontopodium_alpinum "Leontopodium alpinum") (the symbol of Slovene mountaineering), *[Cypripedium calceolus](/wiki/Cypripedium_calceolus "Cypripedium calceolus")*, *[Fritillaria meleagris](/wiki/Fritillaria_meleagris "Fritillaria meleagris")* (snake's head fritillary), and *[Pulsatilla grandis](/wiki/Pulsatilla_grandis "Pulsatilla grandis")* are found.

Slovenia harbors many plants of [ethnobotanically](/wiki/Ethnobotany "Ethnobotany") useful groups. Of 59 known species of ethnobotanical importance, some species such as *[Aconitum napellus](/wiki/Aconitum_napellus "Aconitum napellus")*, *[Cannabis sativa](/wiki/Cannabis_sativa "Cannabis sativa")* and *[Taxus baccata](/wiki/Taxus_baccata "Taxus baccata")* are restricted for use as per the Official Gazette of the Republic of Slovenia.[[124]](#cite_note-127)

## Governance and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Governance and politics")]

Main article: [Politics of Slovenia](/wiki/Politics_of_Slovenia "Politics of Slovenia")

See also: [Foreign relations of Slovenia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Slovenia "Foreign relations of Slovenia")

[![Nataša Pirc Musar](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Nata%C5%A1a_Pirc_Musar_%282023-05-19%29.jpg/250px-Nata%C5%A1a_Pirc_Musar_%282023-05-19%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nata%C5%A1a_Pirc_Musar_(2023-05-19).jpg)

[Nataša Pirc Musar](/wiki/Nata%C5%A1a_Pirc_Musar "Nataša Pirc Musar")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Slovenia "President of Slovenia")

[![Robert Golob](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Novinarska_konferenca_po_sestanku_o_energetski_samooskrbi_-_30.1.2024_-_Robert_Golob_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Novinarska_konferenca_po_sestanku_o_energetski_samooskrbi_-_30.1.2024_-_Robert_Golob_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Novinarska_konferenca_po_sestanku_o_energetski_samooskrbi_-_30.1.2024_-_Robert_Golob_(cropped).jpg)

[Robert Golob](/wiki/Robert_Golob "Robert Golob")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Slovenia "Prime Minister of Slovenia")

Slovenia is a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") republic with a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). According to [International IDEA's](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance") Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Slovenia performs in the high range on overall democratic measures, with particular strengths political representation, including elected government and inclusive suffrage.[[125]](#cite_note-128)[[126]](#cite_note-129)[[127]](#cite_note-130)

### President

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: President")]

The [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") is the [president](/wiki/President_of_Slovenia "President of Slovenia"), who is elected by popular vote and has an important integrative role.[[128]](#cite_note-Furtlehner-131) The president is elected for five years and at maximum for two consecutive terms. The president has a representative role and is the commander-in-chief of the [Slovenian armed forces](/wiki/Military_of_Slovenia "Military of Slovenia").[[129]](#cite_note-132) The president convenes and dissolves Parliament, may express opinions to the National Assembly on any matter of public importance, and has plenary power to call [general elections](/wiki/General_election "General election"), [byelections](/wiki/Byelection "Byelection") and [snap elections](/wiki/Snap_election "Snap election"). The president also nominates prominent state officials, including the prime minister and justices of the Constitutional Court, for the consideration of the National Assembly; conducts Slovenia's foreign relations in consultation with the Government; exercises supreme command and control over the [Armed Forces](/wiki/Slovenian_Armed_Forces "Slovenian Armed Forces"), including the use of significant war powers; confers state honours; and grants pardons, commutations, reprieves, and respites to individuals convicted of criminal offences.

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Government")]

The executive and [administrative](/wiki/Administration_(government) "Administration (government)") authority in Slovenia is held by the [Government of Slovenia](/wiki/Government_of_Slovenia "Government of Slovenia") (**Vlada Republike Slovenije**),[[71]](#cite_note-Borak2004-74) led by the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Slovenia "Prime Minister of Slovenia"). The prime minister chairs the full Council of Ministers or [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)"), who are in turn elected by the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Slovenia) "National Assembly (Slovenia)") (*Državni zbor Republike Slovenije*). The Government is responsible for, among other things, executing the domestic and foreign policies of the state, directing and coordinating the activities of state agencies, preparing and administering the state budget, negotiating and concluding treaties, and implementing both laws passed by the National Assembly and decrees issued by the president.[[130]](#cite_note-:0-133)[[131]](#cite_note-134)

### Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Parliament")]

#### Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Organisation")]

The legislative authority is held by the [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") [Parliament of Slovenia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Slovenia "Parliament of Slovenia"), characterised by an asymmetric duality.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[132]](#cite_note-Prunk2007-135) The bulk of power is concentrated in the National Assembly, which consists of ninety members. Of those, 88 are elected by all the citizens in a system of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), whereas two are elected by the registered members of the [autochthonous](/wiki/Indigenism "Indigenism") Hungarian and Italian [minorities](/wiki/Ethnic_Minorities "Ethnic Minorities"). Elections take place every four years. The [National Council](/wiki/National_Council_(Slovenia) "National Council (Slovenia)") (**Državni svet Republike Slovenije**), consisting of forty members, appointed to represent social, economic, professional and local interest groups, has a limited advisory and control power.[[132]](#cite_note-Prunk2007-135)

#### Partisan composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Partisan composition")]

The 1992–2004 period was marked by the rule of the [Liberal Democracy of Slovenia](/wiki/Liberal_Democracy_of_Slovenia "Liberal Democracy of Slovenia"), which was responsible for gradual transition from the [Titoist](/wiki/Titoism "Titoism") economy to the capitalist market economy. It later attracted much criticism by neo-liberal economists, who demanded a less gradual approach. The party's president [Janez Drnovšek](/wiki/Janez_Drnov%C5%A1ek "Janez Drnovšek"), who served as prime minister between 1992 and 2002, was one of the most influential Slovenian politicians of the 1990s,[[133]](#cite_note-136) alongside President [Milan Kučan](/wiki/Milan_Ku%C4%8Dan "Milan Kučan") (who served between 1990 and 2002).[[134]](#cite_note-137)[[135]](#cite_note-138)

The 2005–2008 period was characterized by over-enthusiasm after joining the EU. During the first term of [Janez Janša](/wiki/Janez_Jan%C5%A1a "Janez Janša")'s government, for the first time after independence, the Slovenian banks saw their [loan-deposit ratios](/wiki/Loan-deposit_ratio "Loan-deposit ratio") veering out of control. There was over-borrowing from foreign banks and then over-crediting of customers, including local [business magnates](/wiki/Business_magnate "Business magnate"). After the onset of the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") and [European sovereign-debt crisis](/wiki/European_sovereign-debt_crisis "European sovereign-debt crisis"), the left-wing coalition that replaced Janša's government in the 2008 elections, had to face the consequences of the 2005–2008 over-borrowing. Attempts to implement reforms that would help economic recovery were met by student protesters, led by a student who later became a member of [Janez Janša](/wiki/Janez_Jan%C5%A1a "Janez Janša")'s [SDS](/wiki/Slovenska_demokratska_stranka "Slovenska demokratska stranka"), and by the trade unions. The proposed reforms were postponed in a referendum. The left-wing government was ousted with a vote of no confidence.

In March 2020, Janez Janša became prime minister for third time in the new coalition government of SDS, the [Modern Centre Party](/wiki/Modern_Centre_Party "Modern Centre Party") (SMC), [New Slovenia](/wiki/New_Slovenia "New Slovenia") (NSi) and Pensioners' Party (DeSUS).[[136]](#cite_note-139) Janez Janša was known as a right-wing populist and a supporter of US President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") and right-wing Prime Minister [Viktor Orban](/wiki/Viktor_Orb%C3%A1n "Viktor Orbán") of Hungary.[[137]](#cite_note-140)[[138]](#cite_note-141) In April 2022, liberal opposition, the [Freedom Movement](/wiki/Freedom_Movement_(Slovenia) "Freedom Movement (Slovenia)"), won the parliamentary [election](/wiki/2022_Slovenian_parliamentary_election "2022 Slovenian parliamentary election"). The Freedom Movement won 34.5% of the vote, compared with 23.6% for Janša's [Slovenian Democratic party](/wiki/Slovenian_Democratic_Party "Slovenian Democratic Party").[[139]](#cite_note-142) On 25 May 2022, Slovenia's parliament voted to appoint the leader of Freedom Movement, [Robert Golob](/wiki/Robert_Golob "Robert Golob"), as the new [Prime Minister of Slovenia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Slovenia "Prime Minister of Slovenia").[[140]](#cite_note-143)

### Judiciary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Judiciary")]

Main articles: [Judiciary of Slovenia](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Slovenia "Judiciary of Slovenia") and [Law enforcement in Slovenia](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Slovenia "Law enforcement in Slovenia")

[Administration of justice](/wiki/Administration_of_justice "Administration of justice") is the responsibility of Slovenia's [constitutional](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Slovenia "Constitutional Court of Slovenia"), [supreme](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Slovenia "Supreme Court of Slovenia"), [inferior](/wiki/Inferior_court "Inferior court") and [trial courts](/wiki/Trial_court "Trial court"), whose judges are appointed on the basis of merit for terms of good behavior. [Judicial power](/wiki/Judicial_power "Judicial power") in Slovenia is exercised by these courts of competent jurisdiction, alongside specialised courts that deal with matters relating to specific legal areas. The Constitutional Court, composed of nine justices nominated by the president and confirmed by the National Assembly for nine-year terms, decides on the conformity of laws and regulations with the Constitution. Its power of [constitutional review](/wiki/Constitutionality "Constitutionality") extends to assuring laws and regulations conform with the general principles of international law and with ratified international agreements.[[141]](#cite_note-144)[[142]](#cite_note-145)[[143]](#cite_note-146) All other judges are appointed by the Judicial Council, an [independent public body](/wiki/Council_of_the_judiciary "Council of the judiciary") responsible for the [administration of the courts](/wiki/Judicial_administration "Judicial administration"), including the recruitment of judges qualified by merit and their discipline.[[144]](#cite_note-147)[[145]](#cite_note-148) Five out of the 11 council members are nominated by the president, which the remainder elected by and from amongst sitting judges. Presidential nominees must be university professors of law, senior counsel, or attorneys accepted to the State Bar.[[146]](#cite_note-149)

The [Supreme State Prosecutor's Office](/wiki/Public_prosecutor%27s_office "Public prosecutor's office"), led by the state prosecutor general, is an independent state authority responsible for prosecuting those suspected of committing criminal offences against the Slovenian state.[[147]](#cite_note-150) The state prosecutor general is nominated by the Government and confirmed by the National Assembly for a six-year term.[[148]](#cite_note-151) Together with all state prosecutors employed by the Supreme State Prosecutor's Office and the eleven district state prosecutor's offices, the state prosecutor general is subject to the professional oversight of the State Prosecutorial Council, a regulatory body.[[149]](#cite_note-152)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Military")]

Main article: [Military of Slovenia](/wiki/Military_of_Slovenia "Military of Slovenia")

The [Slovenian Armed Forces](/wiki/Slovenian_Armed_Forces "Slovenian Armed Forces") provide military defence independently or within an alliance, in accordance with international agreements.
