Since conscription was abolished in 2003, it is organized as a fully professional [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army").[[150]](#cite_note-153) The [Commander-in-Chief](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief "Commander-in-Chief") is the [President of the Republic of Slovenia](/wiki/President_of_Slovenia "President of Slovenia"), while operational command is in the domain of the [Chief of the General Staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_General_Staff_(Slovenia) "Chief of the General Staff (Slovenia)") of the Slovenian Armed Forces. In 2016, military spending was an estimated 0.91% of the country's GDP. Since joining [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the Slovenian Armed Forces have taken a more active part in supporting international peace. They have participated in peace support operations and humanitarian activities. Among others, Slovenian soldiers are part of the international forces serving in [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan").[[151]](#cite_note-154) According to the 2024 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index"), the country is the 9th most peaceful country in the world.[[152]](#cite_note-155)

### Administrative divisions and traditional regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Administrative divisions and traditional regions")]

Main articles: [Statistical regions of Slovenia](/wiki/Statistical_regions_of_Slovenia "Statistical regions of Slovenia") and [Municipalities of Slovenia](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Slovenia "Municipalities of Slovenia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Slokfor.jpg/250px-Slokfor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slokfor.jpg)

[(SAF), Slovenian KFOR](/wiki/Slovenian_Armed_Forces "Slovenian Armed Forces") contingent in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo")

#### Municipalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Municipalities")]

Officially, Slovenia is subdivided into 212 municipalities (twelve of which have the status of urban municipalities). The municipalities are the only bodies of local autonomy in Slovenia. Each municipality is headed by a mayor (*župan*), elected every four years by popular vote, and a municipal council (*občinski svet*). In the majority of municipalities, the municipal council is elected through the system of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"); only a few smaller municipalities use the [plurality voting system](/wiki/Plurality_voting_system "Plurality voting system"). In the urban municipalities, the municipal councils are called town (or city) councils.[[153]](#cite_note-www2.gov.si-156) Every municipality also has a Head of the Municipal Administration (*načelnik občinske uprave*), appointed by the mayor, who is responsible for the functioning of the local administration.[[153]](#cite_note-www2.gov.si-156)

#### Administrative Units (*upravne enote*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Administrative Units (upravne enote)")]

There is no official intermediate unit between the municipalities and the Republic of Slovenia. The 58 "Administrative Units" (*upravne enote*), are only subdivisions of the national government administration and are named after their respective bases of government offices.[[154]](#cite_note-157)

For EU statistical purposes, these 58 administrative units (upravne enote) were classified "local administrative unit 1" (LAU 1).

They are the continuation of the 62 municipalities before 1994 (Ljubljana was until then split in five municipalities: Bežigrad, Center, Moste-Polje, Šiška and Vič-Rudnik)

The [88 electoral districts of Slovenia](/wiki/List_of_constituencies_in_Slovenia "List of constituencies in Slovenia") are still named after these 62 old municipalities.

#### Traditional regions and identities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Traditional regions and identities")]

Slovenia's traditional regions are based on the former [Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") [crown lands](/wiki/Crown_land "Crown land"), which included [Carniola](/wiki/Carniola "Carniola"), [Carinthia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Carinthia "Duchy of Carinthia"), [Styria](/wiki/Styria_(duchy) "Styria (duchy)"), and the [Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral"). Stronger than with either Carniola as a whole, or with Slovenia as a state, Slovenes tend to identify themselves with the traditional regions of the [Slovene Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral"), [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje"), and traditional (sub)regions, such as Upper Carniola, Lower Carniola, and, to a lesser extent, Inner Carniola.[[155]](#cite_note-Repe_deželani_ali_državljani-158)

The capital city Ljubljana was historically the administrative seat of Carniola and belonged to [Inner Carniola](/wiki/Inner_Carniola "Inner Carniola"),[[156]](#cite_note-Mehle-159) except for the Šentvid district, which was in [Upper Carniola](/wiki/Upper_Carniola "Upper Carniola") and also where the border between German-annexed territory and the Italian [Province of Ljubljana](/wiki/Province_of_Ljubljana "Province of Ljubljana") was during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War").[[156]](#cite_note-Mehle-159)

#### Statistical regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Statistical regions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Slovenia%2C_administrative_divisions_-_Nmbrs_%28statistical_regions%29_-_colored.svg/330px-Slovenia%2C_administrative_divisions_-_Nmbrs_%28statistical_regions%29_-_colored.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Slovenia,_administrative_divisions_-_Nmbrs_(statistical_regions)_-_colored.svg)

Statistical regions: 1. Gorizia, 2. Upper Carniola, 3. Carinthia, 4. Drava, 5. Mura, 6. Central Slovenia, 7. Central Sava, 8. Savinja, 9. Coastal–Karst, 10. Inner Carniola–Karst, 11. Southeast Slovenia, 12. Lower Sava

The 12 *statistical regions* have no administrative function and are subdivided into two macroregions for the purpose of the [Regional policy of the European Union](/wiki/Regional_policy_of_the_European_Union "Regional policy of the European Union").[[157]](#cite_note-160)
These two macroregions are:

* [Eastern Slovenia](/wiki/Eastern_Slovenia "Eastern Slovenia") (*Vzhodna Slovenija* – SI01), which groups the Mura, Drava, Carinthia, Savinja, Central Sava, Lower Sava, Southeast Slovenia, and Inner Carniola–Karst statistical regions.
* [Western Slovenia](/wiki/Western_Slovenia "Western Slovenia") (*Zahodna Slovenija* – SI02), which groups the Central Slovenia, Upper Carniola, Gorizia, and Coastal–Karst statistical regions.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Slovenia](/wiki/Economy_of_Slovenia "Economy of Slovenia")

Slovenia has a [developed economy](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") and is the richest Slavic country by GDP per capita.[[158]](#cite_note-rich2-161) Slovenia is also among the top global economies in terms of [human capital](/wiki/Human_capital "Human capital").[[159]](#cite_note-hdi-162) It is the most developed transition country with an old [mining](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining")-[industrial](/wiki/Industrial_society "Industrial society") tradition, [chemical industry](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry"), and developed [service](/wiki/Service_economy "Service economy") activities.
Slovenia was in the beginning of 2007 the first new member to introduce the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") as its currency, replacing the [tolar](/wiki/Slovenian_tolar "Slovenian tolar"). Since 2010, it has been member of the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development").[[160]](#cite_note-VE2012-03-15-163)[[161]](#cite_note-164) There is a big difference in prosperity between the various regions. The economically wealthiest regions are the [Central Slovenia](/wiki/Central_Slovenia_Statistical_Region "Central Slovenia Statistical Region") region, which includes the capital [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana") and the [western Slovenian regions](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral") (the [Gorizia](/wiki/Gorizia_Statistical_Region "Gorizia Statistical Region") and [Coastal–Karst](/wiki/Coastal%E2%80%93Karst_Statistical_Region "Coastal–Karst Statistical Region") Statistical Regions), while the least wealthy regions are the [Mura](/wiki/Mura_Statistical_Region "Mura Statistical Region"), [Central Sava](/wiki/Central_Sava_Statistical_Region "Central Sava Statistical Region"), and [Littoral–Inner Carniola](/wiki/Littoral%E2%80%93Inner_Carniola_Statistical_Region "Littoral–Inner Carniola Statistical Region") Statistical Regions.[[162]](#cite_note-bslo-165)

### Economic growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Economic growth")]

|  |  |

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(April 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/GDP_per_capita_development_in_Slovenia.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_development_in_Slovenia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_in_Slovenia.svg)

GDP per capita development in Slovenia

In 2004–06, the economy grew on average by nearly 5% a year in Slovenia; in 2007, it expanded by almost 7%. The growth surge was fuelled by debt, particularly among firms, and especially in construction. The [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") and [European sovereign-debt crisis](/wiki/European_sovereign-debt_crisis "European sovereign-debt crisis") had a significant impact on the domestic economy.[[163]](#cite_note-Slovenia's_Economy:_Next_in_Line-166) The construction industry was severely hit in 2010 and 2011.[[164]](#cite_note-ST2012-03-13-167)

In 2009, Slovenian [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita") shrank by 8%, the biggest decline in the European Union after the [Baltic countries](/wiki/Baltic_countries "Baltic countries") and Finland. An increasing burden for the Slovenian economy has been its rapidly aging population.[[165]](#cite_note-CNBC2012-01-23-168)

In August 2012, the year-on-year contraction was 0.8%; however, 0.2% growth was recorded in the first quarter (in relation to the quarter before, after data was adjusted according to season and working days).[[166]](#cite_note-169) Year-on-year contraction has been attributed to the fall in domestic consumption and the slowdown in export growth. The decrease in domestic consumption has been attributed to the fiscal [austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity"), to the freeze on budget expenditure in the final months of 2011,[[167]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-170) to the failure of the efforts to implement [economic reforms](/wiki/Microeconomic_reform "Microeconomic reform"), to inappropriate financing, and to the decrease in exports.[[168]](#cite_note-RTV2012-02-29-171)

Due to the effects of the crisis, it was expected that several banks had to be bailed out by EU funds in 2013; however, needed capital was able to be covered by the country's own funds. Fiscal actions and legislations aiming on the reduction of spendings as well as several privatisations supported an economic recovery as from 2014.[[169]](#cite_note-172) The real economic growth rate was at 2.5% in 2016 and accelerated to 5% in 2017.[[170]](#cite_note-auto-173) The construction sector has seen a recent increase,[[170]](#cite_note-auto-173) and the tourism industry is expected to have continuous rising numbers.[[171]](#cite_note-174)

Since 2017, Slovenia has experienced moderate economic growth, with GDP growth averaging around 2% per year between 2017 and 2019. However, like many other countries, Slovenia's economy has been impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, with a contraction of around 5% in 2020. Overall, Slovenia's economy is relatively small but open and has shown resilience in recent years.

Slovenia's manufacturing sector is one of the largest contributors to the country's economy, accounting for around 25% of GDP. The country has a strong tradition in manufacturing, particularly in the areas of automotive and electrical engineering. Other important sectors include services, which account for around 65% of GDP, and agriculture, forestry, and fishing, which account for around 2% of GDP.

Slovenia is a highly export-oriented economy, with exports accounting for around 80% of GDP. The country's main export partners are other European countries, particularly Germany, Italy, and Austria. Key exports include machinery and transport equipment, manufactured goods, and chemicals.

The government of Slovenia has implemented a range of policies aimed at promoting economic growth and development. These include efforts to attract foreign investment, reduce red tape, and increase investment in research and development. Slovenia was ranked 35th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[172]](#cite_note-175)[[173]](#cite_note-176) The country has also introduced reforms aimed at improving the efficiency of its labor market and increasing the flexibility of its economy. The government's approach to consulting [business associations](/wiki/Trade_association "Trade association") has been noted by the European Commission as a good practice example.[[174]](#cite_note-177)

### Services and industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Services and industry")]

Almost two-thirds of people are employed in services, and over one-third in industry and construction.[[175]](#cite_note-CIA-178) Slovenia benefits from a well-educated workforce, well-developed infrastructure, and its location at the crossroads of major trade routes.[[160]](#cite_note-VE2012-03-15-163)

The level of [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment") (FDI) per capita in Slovenia is one of the lowest in the EU,[[160]](#cite_note-VE2012-03-15-163) and the labor productivity and the competitiveness of the Slovenian economy is still significantly below the EU average.[[176]](#cite_note-179)[[177]](#cite_note-180) Taxes are relatively high, the [labor market](/wiki/Labor_market "Labor market") is seen by business interests as being inflexible, and industries are losing sales to China, India, and elsewhere.[[178]](#cite_note-181)

High level of openness makes Slovenia extremely sensitive to economic conditions in its main trading partners and changes in its international price competitiveness.[[179]](#cite_note-182) The main industries are motor vehicles, electric and electronic equipment, machinery, [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceutical "Pharmaceutical"), and fuels.[[160]](#cite_note-VE2012-03-15-163) Examples of major Slovenian companies operating in Slovenia include the home appliance manufacturer [Gorenje](/wiki/Gorenje "Gorenje"), the pharmaceutical companies [Krka](/wiki/Krka_(company) "Krka (company)") and Lek ([Novartis](/wiki/Novartis "Novartis")' subsidiary), the oil distributing company [Petrol Group](/wiki/Petrol_Group "Petrol Group"), energy distribution companys GEN, GEN-I, HSE and [Revoz](/wiki/Revoz "Revoz"), a manufacturing subsidiary of [Renault](/wiki/Renault "Renault").[[180]](#cite_note-183)[[181]](#cite_note-184)[[182]](#cite_note-185)

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Energy")]

Further information: [Energy in Slovenia](/wiki/Energy_in_Slovenia "Energy in Slovenia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Nuklearna_elektrarna_Kr%C5%A1ko.jpg/250px-Nuklearna_elektrarna_Kr%C5%A1ko.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nuklearna_elektrarna_Kr%C5%A1ko.jpg)

Krško Nuclear Power Plant, 696 MW

In 2018, the net energy production was 12,262 GWh and consumption was 14,501 GWh. Hydroelectric plants produced 4,421 GWh, thermal plants produced 4,049 GWh, and the [Krško Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Kr%C5%A1ko_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Krško Nuclear Power Plant") produced 2,742 GWh (50% share that goes to Slovenia; other 50% goes to Croatia due to joint ownership).
Domestic electricity consumption was covered 84.6% by domestic production; the percentage is decreasing from year to year meaning Slovenia is more and more dependent on electricity imports.[[183]](#cite_note-agencija-186)

A new 600 MW block of [Šoštanj thermal power plant](/wiki/%C5%A0o%C5%A1tanj#Power_plant "Šoštanj") finished construction and went online in the autumn of 2014.[[184]](#cite_note-187) The new 39.5 MW HE Krško hydro power plant was finished in 2013.[[185]](#cite_note-188) The 41.5 MW HE Brežice and 30.5 MW HE Mokrice hydro power plants were built on the [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava") River in 2018 and the construction of ten more hydropower plants with a cumulative capacity of 338 MW is planned to be finished by 2030. A large [pumped-storage hydro power plant](/wiki/Pumped-storage_hydroelectricity "Pumped-storage hydroelectricity") Kozjak on the [Drava](/wiki/Drava "Drava") River is in the planning stage.

At the end of 2018, at least 295 MWp of [photovoltaic modules](/wiki/Photovoltaics "Photovoltaics") and 31,4 MW of [biogas powerplants](/wiki/Biogas_power_plant "Biogas power plant") were installed. Compared to 2017, renewable energy sources contributed 5.6 percentage points more into whole energy consumption. There is interest to add more production in the area of solar and wind energy sources (subsidising schemes are increasing economic feasibility), but microlocation settlement procedures take enormous toll on the efficiency of this initiative (nature preservation vs. energy production facilities dilemma).[[183]](#cite_note-agencija-186)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![Postojna Cave](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Postojna_Cave._2012-07-26_13-41-13.jpg/250px-Postojna_Cave._2012-07-26_13-41-13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Postojna_Cave._2012-07-26_13-41-13.jpg)

[Postojna Cave](/wiki/Postojna_Cave "Postojna Cave")

Main article: [Tourism in Slovenia](/wiki/Tourism_in_Slovenia "Tourism in Slovenia")

Slovenia offers tourists a wide variety of natural and cultural amenities. Different forms of tourism have developed. The tourist gravitational area is considerably large, however the tourist market is small. There has been no large-scale tourism and no acute environmental pressures;[[186]](#cite_note-189) in 2017, [National Geographic](/wiki/National_Geographic "National Geographic") Traveller's Magazine declared Slovenia as the country with the world's most [sustainable tourism](/wiki/Sustainable_tourism "Sustainable tourism").[[187]](#cite_note-190) The nation's capital, [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana"), has many important [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") and [Vienna Secession](/wiki/Vienna_Secession "Vienna Secession") buildings, with several important works of the native born architect [Jože Plečnik](/wiki/Jo%C5%BEe_Ple%C4%8Dnik "Jože Plečnik").[[188]](#cite_note-191)

At the northwestern corner of the country lie the [Julian Alps](/wiki/Julian_Alps "Julian Alps") with [Lake Bled](/wiki/Lake_Bled "Lake Bled") and the [Soča](/wiki/So%C4%8Da "Soča") Valley, as well as the nation's highest peak, [Mount Triglav](/wiki/Mount_Triglav "Mount Triglav") in the middle of [Triglav National Park](/wiki/Triglav_National_Park "Triglav National Park"). Other mountain ranges include [Kamnik–Savinja Alps](/wiki/Kamnik%E2%80%93Savinja_Alps "Kamnik–Savinja Alps"), the [Karawanks](/wiki/Karawanks "Karawanks"), and [Pohorje](/wiki/Pohorje "Pohorje"), popular with skiers and hikers.[[189]](#cite_note-192)

The [Karst Plateau](/wiki/Karst_Plateau "Karst Plateau") in the [Slovene Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral") gave its name to [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst"), a landscape shaped by water dissolving the carbonate bedrock, forming caves. The best-known caves are [Postojna Cave](/wiki/Postojna_Cave "Postojna Cave") and the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site")-listed [Škocjan Caves](/wiki/%C5%A0kocjan_Caves "Škocjan Caves"). The region of [Slovenian Istria](/wiki/Slovenian_Istria "Slovenian Istria") meets the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea"), where the most important historical monument is the [Venetian Gothic](/wiki/Venetian_Gothic_architecture "Venetian Gothic architecture") Mediterranean town of [Piran](/wiki/Piran "Piran") while the settlement of [Portorož](/wiki/Portoro%C5%BE "Portorož") attracts crowds in summer.[[190]](#cite_note-193)

[![Lake Bled](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/View_of_Bled_Island_%286%29.jpg/250px-View_of_Bled_Island_%286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_Bled_Island_(6).jpg)

[Lake Bled](/wiki/Lake_Bled "Lake Bled") with its island

The hills around Slovenia's second-largest city, [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor"), are renowned for their wine-making. The northeastern part of the country is rich with spas,[[191]](#cite_note-194) with [Rogaška Slatina](/wiki/Roga%C5%A1ka_Slatina "Rogaška Slatina"), [Radenci](/wiki/Radenci "Radenci"), [Čatež ob Savi](/wiki/%C4%8Cate%C5%BE_ob_Savi "Čatež ob Savi"), [Dobrna](/wiki/Dobrna "Dobrna"), and [Moravske Toplice](/wiki/Moravske_Toplice "Moravske Toplice") growing in importance in the last two decades.[[192]](#cite_note-195)

Other popular tourist destinations include the historic cities of [Ptuj](/wiki/Ptuj "Ptuj") and [Škofja Loka](/wiki/%C5%A0kofja_Loka "Škofja Loka"), and several castles, such as [Predjama Castle](/wiki/Predjama_Castle "Predjama Castle").[[193]](#cite_note-196)[[194]](#cite_note-197)

Important parts of tourism in Slovenia include congress and [gambling tourism](/wiki/Gambling_tourism "Gambling tourism"). Slovenia is the country with the highest percentage of [casinos](/wiki/Casino "Casino") per 1,000 inhabitants in the European Union.[[195]](#cite_note-198) [Perla](/w/index.php?title=Perla_hotel_and_casino&action=edit&redlink=1 "Perla hotel and casino (page does not exist)") in [Nova Gorica](/wiki/Nova_Gorica "Nova Gorica") is the largest casino in the region.[[196]](#cite_note-199)

Most of foreign tourists to Slovenia come from the key European markets: [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), followed by [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland").[[197]](#cite_note-200) European tourists create more than 90% of Slovenia's tourist income. In 2016, Slovenia was declared the world's first green country by the Netherlands-based organization Green Destinations.[[198]](#cite_note-201) On being declared the most sustainable country in 2016, Slovenia had a big part to play at the [ITB Berlin](/wiki/ITB_Berlin "ITB Berlin") to promote sustainable tourism.

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Slovenia](/wiki/Transport_in_Slovenia "Transport in Slovenia")

Geography has dictated transport routes in Slovenia. Significant mountain ranges, major rivers and proximity to the Danube played roles in the development of the area's transportation corridors. One recent particular advantage are the [Pan-European transport corridors](/wiki/Pan-European_transport_corridor "Pan-European transport corridor") [V](/w/index.php?title=Pan-European_transport_corridor_V&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pan-European transport corridor V (page does not exist)") (the fastest link between the North Adriatic, and Central and Eastern Europe) and [X](/wiki/Pan-European_Corridor_X "Pan-European Corridor X") (linking Central Europe with the Balkans). This gives it a special position in the European social, economic and cultural integration and restructuring.[[199]](#cite_note-UKOM2000-11-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Motorways-SLO-map_2018.jpg/500px-Motorways-SLO-map_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motorways-SLO-map_2018.jpg)

Motorways in Slovenia in August 2020

#### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Roads")]

The road freight and passenger transport constitutes the largest part of transport in Slovenia at 80%.[[200]](#cite_note-MW2009SORS-203) Personal cars are much more popular than public road passenger transport, which has significantly declined.[[200]](#cite_note-MW2009SORS-203)[[201]](#cite_note-204) Slovenia has a very high [highway and motorway density](/wiki/Highways_in_Slovenia "Highways in Slovenia") compared to the European Union average.[[202]](#cite_note-ECORYS2011-205) The highway system, the construction of which was accelerated after 1994,[[203]](#cite_note-Oplotnik2004-206) has slowly but steadily transformed Slovenia into a large [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation").[[204]](#cite_note-207) Other state roads have been rapidly deteriorating because of neglect and the overall increase in traffic.[[202]](#cite_note-ECORYS2011-205)

#### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Railways")]

Main article: [Slovenian Railways](/wiki/Slovenian_Railways "Slovenian Railways")

The existing Slovenian railways are out-of-date and have difficulty competing with the motorway network; partially also as a result of dispersed population settlement.[[205]](#cite_note-ARSO420-208) Due to this fact and the projected increase in traffic through the port of [Koper](/wiki/Koper "Koper"), which is primarily by train, a second rail on the Koper-Divača route is in early stages of starting construction.[[206]](#cite_note-209) With a lack of financial assets, maintenance and modernisation of the Slovenian railway network have been neglected.[[207]](#cite_note-UNIMB2011-04-210) Due to the out-of-date infrastructure, the share of the railway freight transport has been in decline in Slovenia.[[208]](#cite_note-ARS416-211) The railway passenger transport has been recovering after a large drop in the 1990s.[[208]](#cite_note-ARS416-211) The Pan-European railway corridors V and X, and several other major European rail lines intersect in Slovenia.[[207]](#cite_note-UNIMB2011-04-210)

#### Ports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Ports")]

The major Slovenian port is the [Port of Koper](/wiki/Port_of_Koper "Port of Koper"). It is the largest Northern Adriatic port in terms of container transport,[[209]](#cite_note-212) with almost 590,000 [TEUs](/wiki/Twenty-foot_equivalent_unit "Twenty-foot equivalent unit") annually[[210]](#cite_note-213) and lines to all major world ports.[[211]](#cite_note-ECONSHIP2011-214)[[212]](#cite_note-AH2008-16-3GA-215) It is much closer to destinations east of the [Suez](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal") than the ports of Northern Europe.[[211]](#cite_note-ECONSHIP2011-214) In addition, the maritime passenger traffic mostly takes place in Koper.[[213]](#cite_note-RR21-216) Two smaller ports used for the international passenger transport as well as cargo transport are located in [Izola](/wiki/Izola "Izola") and [Piran](/wiki/Piran "Piran"). Passenger transport mainly takes place with Italy and Croatia.[[214]](#cite_note-ReNPRP-217) [Splošna plovba](/w/index.php?title=Splo%C5%A1na_plovba&action=edit&redlink=1 "Splošna plovba (page does not exist)"),[[215]](#cite_note-218) the only Slovenian shipping company, transports freight and is active only in foreign ports.[[213]](#cite_note-RR21-216)

#### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Air")]

Air transport in Slovenia is very low,[[208]](#cite_note-ARS416-211) but has significantly grown since 1991.[[216]](#cite_note-RR26-219) Of the three international airports in Slovenia, [Ljubljana Jože Pučnik Airport](/wiki/Ljubljana_Jo%C5%BEe_Pu%C4%8Dnik_Airport "Ljubljana Jože Pučnik Airport") in central Slovenia is the busiest,[[216]](#cite_note-RR26-219) with connections to many major European destinations.[[217]](#cite_note-220) The [Maribor Edvard Rusjan Airport](/wiki/Maribor_Edvard_Rusjan_Airport "Maribor Edvard Rusjan Airport") is located in the eastern part of the country and the [Portorož Airport](/wiki/Portoro%C5%BE_Airport "Portorož Airport") in the west.[[216]](#cite_note-RR26-219) The state-owned [Adria Airways](/wiki/Adria_Airways "Adria Airways") was the largest Slovenian airline; however in 2019 it declared bankruptcy and ceased operations.[[216]](#cite_note-RR26-219) Since 2003, several new carriers have entered the market, mainly low-cost airlines.[[202]](#cite_note-ECORYS2011-205) The only Slovenian [military airport](/wiki/Military_airbase "Military airbase") is the [Cerklje ob Krki Air Base](/wiki/Cerklje_ob_Krki_Air_Base "Cerklje ob Krki Air Base") in the southwest.[[218]](#cite_note-221) There are also 12 [public airports](/wiki/Public_airport "Public airport") in Slovenia.[[216]](#cite_note-RR26-219)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of Slovenia](/wiki/Demographics_of_Slovenia "Demographics of Slovenia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Population_density_in_Slovenia.png/330px-Population_density_in_Slovenia.png)](/wiki/File:Population_density_in_Slovenia.png)

Population density in Slovenia by [municipality](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Slovenia "Municipalities of Slovenia"). The four main urban areas are visible: Ljubljana and Kranj (centre), Maribor (northeast) and the [Slovene Istria](/wiki/Slovene_Istria "Slovene Istria") (southwest)

The population of Slovenia as of October 2025 is 2,133,852.[[4]](#cite_note-Statistical_Office_of_Slovenia-5) With 105 inhabitants per square kilometer (272/sq mi), Slovenia ranks low among the European countries in population density (compared to 402/km2 (1,040/mi2) for the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") or 195/km2 (510/mi2) for Italy). The [Inner Carniola–Karst Statistical Region](/wiki/Inner_Carniola%E2%80%93Karst_Statistical_Region "Inner Carniola–Karst Statistical Region") has the lowest population density while the [Central Slovenia Statistical Region](/wiki/Central_Slovenia_Statistical_Region "Central Slovenia Statistical Region") has the highest.[[219]](#cite_note-222)

Slovenia is among the European countries with the most pronounced ageing of its population, ascribable to a low birth rate and increasing life expectancy.[[220]](#cite_note-223) Almost all Slovenian inhabitants older than 64 are retired, with no significant difference between the genders.[[221]](#cite_note-Hoff2011-224) The working-age group is diminishing in spite of immigration.[[222]](#cite_note-225) The proposal to raise the retirement age from the current 57 for women and 58 for men was rejected in a [referendum in 2011](/wiki/June_2011_Slovenian_referendum "June 2011 Slovenian referendum").[[165]](#cite_note-CNBC2012-01-23-168) In addition, the difference among the genders regarding life expectancy is still significant.[[221]](#cite_note-Hoff2011-224) The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR) in 2024 was estimated at 1.6 children born/woman, which is lower than the replacement rate of 2.1.[[223]](#cite_note-226) The majority of children are born to unmarried women (in 2023, 56.5% of all births were outside of marriage).[[224]](#cite_note-227)
In 2023, life expectancy at birth was 82 years (79.1 years male, and 85 years female).[[225]](#cite_note-228)

In 2020, the [suicide rate](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_suicide_rate "List of countries by suicide rate") in Slovenia was 17 per 100,000 persons per year, which places Slovenia among the highest ranked European countries.[[226]](#cite_note-stat2009suicide-229) Nonetheless, from 2000 until 2010, the rate has decreased by about 30%. The differences between regions and the genders are pronounced.[[227]](#cite_note-230)

### Ethnic structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Ethnic structure")]

The largest ethnic groups in Slovenia are [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") (83.1%), [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs_in_Slovenia "Serbs in Slovenia") (2.0%), [Croats](/wiki/Croats_of_Slovenia "Croats of Slovenia")
(1.8%), [Bosniaks](/wiki/Bosniaks_of_Slovenia "Bosniaks of Slovenia") (1.6%), [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims_(ethnic_group) "Muslims (ethnic group)") (0.5%), [Bosnians](/wiki/Bosnians "Bosnians") (0.4%), [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians") (0.3%), [Albanians](/wiki/Albanians_in_Slovenia "Albanians in Slovenia") (0.3%) and [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Slovenia "Romani people in Slovenia") (0.2%). Other ethnic groups in Slovenia include Macedonians, Italians, Montenegrins and Germans.[[228]](#cite_note-231)

### Urbanisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Urbanisation")]

Main article: [List of cities and towns in Slovenia](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Slovenia "List of cities and towns in Slovenia")

Depending on definition, between 65% and 79% of people live in wider urban areas.[[229]](#cite_note-232) According to [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") definition of rural areas none of the [Slovene statistical regions](/wiki/Statistical_regions_of_Slovenia "Statistical regions of Slovenia") is mostly urbanised, meaning that 15% or less of the population lives in rural communities. According to this definition statistical regions are classified:

* mostly rural regions: [Mura](/wiki/Mura_Statistical_Region "Mura Statistical Region"), [Drava](/wiki/Drava_Statistical_Region "Drava Statistical Region"), [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia_Statistical_Region "Carinthia Statistical Region"), [Savinja](/wiki/Savinja_Statistical_Region "Savinja Statistical Region"), [Lower Sava](/wiki/Lower_Sava_Statistical_Region "Lower Sava Statistical Region"), [Littoral–Inner Carniola](/wiki/Littoral%E2%80%93Inner_Carniola_Statistical_Region "Littoral–Inner Carniola Statistical Region"), [Gorizia](/wiki/Gorizia_Statistical_Region "Gorizia Statistical Region"), [Southeast Slovenia](/wiki/Southeast_Slovenia_Statistical_Region "Southeast Slovenia Statistical Region")
* moderately rural regions: [Central Sava](/wiki/Central_Sava_Statistical_Region "Central Sava Statistical Region"), [Upper Carniola](/wiki/Upper_Carniola_Statistical_Region "Upper Carniola Statistical Region"), [Coastal–Karst](/wiki/Coastal%E2%80%93Karst_Statistical_Region "Coastal–Karst Statistical Region"), [Central Slovenia](/wiki/Central_Slovenia_Statistical_Region "Central Slovenia Statistical Region").[[230]](#cite_note-233)

The only large town is the capital, Ljubljana. Other (medium-sized) towns include Maribor, Celje, and Kranj.[[231]](#cite_note-GV52-2-234)[[232]](#cite_note-235) Overall, there are eleven urban municipalities in Slovenia.

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Languages")]

Main articles: [Languages of Slovenia](/wiki/Languages_of_Slovenia "Languages of Slovenia") and [Slovene language](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language")

The official language in Slovenia is [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), which is a member of the [South Slavic language group](/wiki/South_Slavic_languages "South Slavic languages"). In 2002, Slovene was the native language of around 88% of Slovenia's population according to the census, with more than 92% of the Slovenian population speaking it in their home environment.[[233]](#cite_note-siol.net-236)[[234]](#cite_note-stat.si-237) This statistic ranks Slovenia among the most homogeneous countries in the EU in terms of the share of speakers of the predominant mother tongue.[[235]](#cite_note-Dular-238)

Slovene is a highly diverse Slavic language in terms of [dialects](/wiki/Slovene_dialects "Slovene dialects"),[[236]](#cite_note-MLD_2009-239) with different degrees of mutual intelligibility. Accounts of the number of dialects range from as few as seven[[237]](#cite_note-240)[[238]](#cite_note-241)[[239]](#cite_note-242) dialects, often considered dialect groups or dialect bases that are further subdivided into as many as 50 dialects.[[240]](#cite_note-243) Other sources characterize the number of dialects as nine[[241]](#cite_note-244) or as eight.[[242]](#cite_note-245)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Slovenian_Passport3.jpg/250px-Slovenian_Passport3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slovenian_Passport3.jpg)

Front cover of a bilingual passport in Slovene and Italian

[Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Slovenia "Italian language in Slovenia"), spoken by the respective minorities, enjoy the status of official languages in the ethnically mixed regions along the Hungarian and Italian borders, to the extent that even the passports issued in those areas are bilingual. In 2002 around 0.2% of the Slovenian population spoke Italian and around 0.4% spoke Hungarian as their native language. Hungarian is co-official with Slovene in 30 settlements in 5 municipalities (whereof 3 are officially bilingual). Italian is co-official with Slovene in 25 settlements in 4 municipalities (all of them officially bilingual).

[Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language"),[[243]](#cite_note-246) spoken in 2002 as the native language by 0.2% of people, is a legally protected language in Slovenia. Romani speakers mainly belong to the geographically dispersed and marginalized Roma community.[[244]](#cite_note-247)

German, which used to be the largest minority language in Slovenia prior to World War II (around 4% of the population in 1921), is now the native language of only around 0.08% of the population, the majority of whom are more than 60 years old.[[234]](#cite_note-stat.si-237) [Gottscheerish](/wiki/Gottscheerish "Gottscheerish") or *Granish*, the traditional German dialect of [Gottschee](/wiki/Gottschee "Gottschee") County, faces extinction.[[245]](#cite_note-norwaysi-248)

A significant number of people in Slovenia speak a variant of [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian") ([Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language"), [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), [Bosnian](/wiki/Bosnian_language "Bosnian language"), or [Montenegrin](/wiki/Montenegrin_language "Montenegrin language")) as their native language. These are mostly families who moved to Slovenia from other [former Yugoslav](/wiki/Former_Yugoslav "Former Yugoslav") republics. Altogether, Serbo-Croatian in its different forms is the second natively spoken language in Slovenia with 5.9% of population. In 2002, 0.4% of the Slovenian population declared themselves to be native speakers of [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language") and 0.2% native speakers of [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonian_language "Macedonian language").[[234]](#cite_note-stat.si-237) [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"), the fourth-largest minority language in Slovenia prior to World War II (after German, Hungarian, and Serbo-Croatian), is now the native language of a few hundred residents of Slovenia.[[234]](#cite_note-stat.si-237)

Slovenia ranks among the top European countries in knowledge of foreign languages. The most taught foreign languages are English, German, Italian, French and Spanish. As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit), 92% of the population between the age of 25 and 64 spoke at least one foreign language and around 71.8% of them spoke at least two foreign languages, which was the highest percentage in the European Union.[[246]](#cite_note-249) According to the [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") survey, as of 2005[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit) the majority of Slovenes could speak [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language") (61%) and English (56%).[[247]](#cite_note-EurobarLang-250): 21 A reported 42% of Slovenes could speak German, which was one of the highest percentages outside German-speaking countries.[[247]](#cite_note-EurobarLang-250) Italian is widely spoken on the [Slovenian Coast](/wiki/Slovenian_Istria "Slovenian Istria") and in some other areas of the [Slovene Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral"). Around 15% of Slovenians can speak Italian, which is (according to the Eurobarometer pool) the third-highest percentage in the European Union, after Italy and [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta").[[248]](#cite_note-251)

### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Immigration")]

In 2021, about 13.9% (292,824 people) of the population in Slovenia was born abroad.[[249]](#cite_note-SURS-252) About 86% of the foreign-born population originated from other countries of former Yugoslavia as (in descending order) [Bosnia-Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia-Herzegovina "Bosnia-Herzegovina"), followed by immigrants from [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), and [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia").[[249]](#cite_note-SURS-252)

By the beginning of 2017, there were about 114,438 people with foreign citizenship residing in the country making up 5.5% of the total population. Of these foreigners, 76% had citizenships of the other countries from former Yugoslavia (excluding Croatia). Additionally 16.4% had EU-citizenships and 7.6% had citizenships of other countries.[[249]](#cite_note-SURS-252)

According to the 2002 census, Slovenia's main ethnic group are [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") (83%); however, their share in the total population is continuously decreasing, due to their relatively low [fertility rate](/wiki/Fertility_rate "Fertility rate"). At least 13% (2002) of the population were immigrants from other parts of [Former Yugoslavia](/wiki/Former_Yugoslavia "Former Yugoslavia") and their descendants.[[251]](#cite_note-Medvesek-254) They have settled mainly in cities and suburbanised areas.[[252]](#cite_note-Repolusk-255) Relatively small but protected by the [Constitution of Slovenia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Slovenia "Constitution of Slovenia") are the [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarians_in_Slovenia "Hungarians in Slovenia") and the [Italian ethnic minority](/wiki/Italians_in_Slovenia "Italians in Slovenia").[[253]](#cite_note-MDL2010-256)[[254]](#cite_note-Constitution-257)[[255]](#cite_note-258) A special position is held by the autochthonous and geographically dispersed [Roma ethnic community](/wiki/Roma_in_Slovenia "Roma in Slovenia").[[256]](#cite_note-259)[[257]](#cite_note-260)

The number of people immigrating into Slovenia rose steadily from 1995[[258]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-261) and has been increasing even more rapidly in recent years. After Slovenia joined the EU in 2004, the annual number of immigrants doubled by 2006 and increased by half yet again by 2009.[[259]](#cite_note-262) In 2007, Slovenia had one of the fastest growing [net migration rates](/wiki/Net_migration_rate "Net migration rate") in the European Union.[[258]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-261)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main article: [Religion in Slovenia](/wiki/Religion_in_Slovenia "Religion in Slovenia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Radovljica_Brezje_Bazilika_Sv_Vida_28082012_122.jpg/250px-Radovljica_Brezje_Bazilika_Sv_Vida_28082012_122.jpg)](/wiki/File:Radovljica_Brezje_Bazilika_Sv_Vida_28082012_122.jpg)

The National Shrine Mary Help of Christians at [Brezje](/wiki/Brezje,_Radovljica "Brezje, Radovljica")

Before World War II, 97% of the Slovenian population identified as members of the [Catholic Church in the country](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Slovenia "Catholic Church in Slovenia"), around 2.5% as Lutheran, and around 0.5% of residents identified themselves as members of other denominations.[[234]](#cite_note-stat.si-237) After 1945, the country underwent a process of gradual but steady [secularization](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization"). After a decade of persecution of religions, the Communist regime adopted a policy of relative tolerance towards churches. After 1990, the Catholic Church regained some of its former influence, but Slovenia remains a largely secularized society.

The 2018 Eurobarometer data shows 73.4% of population identifying as Catholic.[[1]](#cite_note-EB2018-2) This fell to 72.1% in the 2019 Eurobarometer survey.[[260]](#cite_note-EB2019-263) According to the Catholic Church data, the Catholic population fell from 78.04% in 2009 to 72.11% in 2019.[[261]](#cite_note-264) The vast majority of Slovenian Catholics belong to the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church"). A small number of [Eastern Catholics](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") live in the [White Carniola](/wiki/White_Carniola "White Carniola") region.[[262]](#cite_note-265)

Religion in Slovenia (2019)[[260]](#cite_note-EB2019-263)

1. [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Slovenia "Catholic Church in Slovenia") 72.1 (69.5%)
2. None 18 (17.4%)
3. [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") 3.7 (3.57%)
4. [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") 0.9 (0.87%)
5. Other Christian 1 (0.96%)
6. [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") 3 (2.89%)
7. Other religion 3 (2.89%)
8. Undeclared 2 (1.93%)

Despite a relatively small number of [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") (less than 1% in 2002), the Protestant legacy is historically significant given that the Slovene standard language and Slovene literature were established by the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"). [Primož Trubar](/wiki/Primo%C5%BE_Trubar "Primož Trubar"), a theologian in the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") tradition, was one of the most influential [Protestant Reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformers "Protestant Reformers") in Slovenia. Protestantism was extinguished in the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation") implemented by the [Habsburg dynasty](/wiki/Habsburg_dynasty "Habsburg dynasty"), which controlled the region. It only survived in the easternmost regions due to protection of Hungarian nobles, who often happened to be [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") themselves. Today, a significant [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") minority lives in the easternmost region of [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje"), where they represent around a fifth of the population and are headed by a bishop with the seat in [Murska Sobota](/wiki/Murska_Sobota "Murska Sobota").[[263]](#cite_note-266)

The third largest denomination, with around 2.2% of the population, is the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), with most adherents belonging to the [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") while a minority belongs to the [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonian_Orthodox_Church "Macedonian Orthodox Church") and other Eastern Orthodox churches.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to the 2002 census, [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the second largest religious denomination in the country, with around 2.4% of the population. Most Slovenian Muslims came from [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia "Bosnia").[[264]](#cite_note-267)

There was a [Slovenian Jewish community](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Slovenia "History of the Jews in Slovenia") before the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust").

In the 2002 around 10% of Slovenes declared themselves [atheists](/wiki/Atheists "Atheists"), another 10% professed no specific denomination, and around 16% declined to answer. According to the [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") Poll 2010,[[130]](#cite_note-:0-133) 32% of Slovenian citizens "believe there is a god", whereas 36% "believe there is some sort of spirit or life force" and 26% "do not believe there is any sort of spirit, god, or life force".[[130]](#cite_note-:0-133)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Slovenia](/wiki/Education_in_Slovenia "Education in Slovenia")

Slovenia's education ranks as the 12th best in the world and 4th best in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), being significantly higher than the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") average, according to the [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment").[[265]](#cite_note-268) Among people age 25 to 64, 12% have attended higher education, while on average Slovenes have 9.6 years of formal education. According to an OECD report, 83% of adults ages 25–64 have earned the equivalent of a high school degree, well above the OECD average of 74%; among 25- to 34-year-olds, the rate is 93%.[[266]](#cite_note-269) According to the 1991 census there is 99.6% [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") in Slovenia. [Lifelong learning](/wiki/Lifelong_learning "Lifelong learning") is also increasing.[[267]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-270)

Responsibility for education oversight at primary and secondary level in Slovenia lies with the Ministry of Education and Sports. After non-compulsory pre-school education, children enter the nine-year primary school at the age of six.[[268]](#cite_note-271) Primary school is divided into three periods, each of three years. In the 2006–2007 academic year, there were 166,000 pupils enrolled in elementary education and more than 13,225 teachers, giving a ratio of one teacher per 12 pupils and 20 pupils per class.

After completing elementary school, nearly all children (more than 98%) go on to secondary education, either vocational, technical, or general secondary programmes ([*gimnazija*](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)")). The last concludes with the [*matura*](/wiki/Matura#Slovenia "Matura"), a comprehensive exam that allows the graduates to enter a university. 84% of secondary school graduates go on to tertiary education.[[267]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-270)

Among several universities in Slovenia, the best ranked is the [University of Ljubljana](/wiki/University_of_Ljubljana "University of Ljubljana"), ranking among the first 500 or the first 3% of the world's best universities according to the [ARWU](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities").[[269]](#cite_note-272)[[270]](#cite_note-273) Two other public universities include the [University of Maribor](/wiki/University_of_Maribor "University of Maribor")[[271]](#cite_note-274) in [Styria](/wiki/Styria_(Slovenia) "Styria (Slovenia)") region and the [University of Primorska](/wiki/University_of_Primorska "University of Primorska") in [Slovene Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral").[[272]](#cite_note-275) In addition, there is a private [University of Nova Gorica](/wiki/University_of_Nova_Gorica "University of Nova Gorica")[[273]](#cite_note-276) and an international [EMUNI University](/wiki/EMUNI_University "EMUNI University").[[274]](#cite_note-277)
