[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Ivan_Grohar_-_Sejalec.jpg/250px-Ivan_Grohar_-_Sejalec.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ivan_Grohar_-_Sejalec.jpg)

*[The Sower](/wiki/The_Sower_(Grohar) "The Sower (Grohar)")* (1907), by the Impressionist painter [Ivan Grohar](/wiki/Ivan_Grohar "Ivan Grohar"), became a [metaphor](/wiki/Metaphor "Metaphor") for Slovenes[[275]](#cite_note-278)[[276]](#cite_note-279) and was a reflection of the transition from a rural to an urban culture.[[277]](#cite_note-280)

### Heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Heritage")]

Slovenia's architectural heritage includes 2,500 churches, 1,000 castles, ruins, and manor houses, farmhouses, and special structures for drying hay, called [hayracks](/wiki/Hayrack "Hayrack") (*kozolci*).[[278]](#cite_note-281)

Four natural and cultural sites in Slovenia are on the [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") list. [Škocjan Caves](/wiki/%C5%A0kocjan_Caves "Škocjan Caves") and its [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst") landscape are a protected site[[279]](#cite_note-282) as the old forests in the area of Goteniški Snežnik and Kočevski Rog in the SE Slovenia. The [Idrija Mercury mining site](/wiki/Heritage_of_Mercury._Almad%C3%A9n_and_Idrija "Heritage of Mercury. Almadén and Idrija") is of world importance, as are the [prehistoric pile dwellings](/wiki/Prehistoric_pile_dwellings_around_the_Alps "Prehistoric pile dwellings around the Alps") in the [Ljubljana Marsh](/wiki/Ljubljana_Marsh "Ljubljana Marsh").[[280]](#cite_note-283)

The most picturesque church for photographers is the medieval and Baroque building on [Bled Island](/wiki/Lake_Bled#Bled_Island "Lake Bled"). Near [Postojna](/wiki/Postojna "Postojna") there is a fortress called [Predjama Castle](/wiki/Predjama_Castle "Predjama Castle"), half hidden in a cave. Museums in Ljubljana and elsewhere feature unique items such as the controversial [Divje Babe flute](/wiki/Divje_Babe_flute "Divje Babe flute"), and the [oldest wheel in the world](/wiki/Ljubljana_Marshes_Wheel "Ljubljana Marshes Wheel"). Ljubljana has medieval, Baroque, Art Nouveau, and modern architecture. The architect [Plečnik](/wiki/Ple%C4%8Dnik "Plečnik")'s architecture and his innovative paths and bridges along the Ljubljanica are notable and on UNESCO tentative list.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main articles: [Slovenian cuisine](/wiki/Slovenian_cuisine "Slovenian cuisine") and [Slovenian wine](/wiki/Slovenian_wine "Slovenian wine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Velika_no%C4%8D_-_jedila_hren_%C5%A1unka_pirhi_potica.jpg/250px-Velika_no%C4%8D_-_jedila_hren_%C5%A1unka_pirhi_potica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Velika_no%C4%8D_-_jedila_hren_%C5%A1unka_pirhi_potica.jpg)

[Potica](/wiki/Slovenian_potica "Slovenian potica") as part of traditional Slovenian Easter breakfast

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Maribor_Zametovka_vine_and_daughter.png/330px-Maribor_Zametovka_vine_and_daughter.png)](/wiki/File:Maribor_Zametovka_vine_and_daughter.png)

The more-than-400-year-old Žametovka vine growing outside the Old Vine House in Maribor. To the right of the vine is a daughter vine taken from a cutting of the old vine.

Slovenian cuisine is a mixture of Central European cuisine (especially [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_cuisine "Austrian cuisine") and [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_cuisine "Hungarian cuisine")) and [Mediterranean cuisine](/wiki/Mediterranean_cuisine "Mediterranean cuisine").

Slovenian cuisine is often characterized as Central European, with cultural actors emphasizing its Austrian and Hungarian influences while distancing it from Balkan culinary traditions.[[281]](#cite_note-284)

In particular, the coastal regions of Slovenia are strongly influenced by Mediterranean culinary traditions. This Mediterranean influence is especially notable in the Istrian peninsula and along the Adriatic coast, where Italian and Croatian culinary traditions blend with local Slovenian flavors.[[282]](#cite_note-285)

Historically, Slovenian cuisine was divided into town, farmhouse, cottage, castle, parsonage and monastic cuisines. Due to the variety of Slovenian cultural and natural landscapes, there are more than 40 distinct regional cuisines.[[283]](#cite_note-286)

[Ethnologically](/wiki/Ethnology "Ethnology"), the most characteristic Slovene dishes were one-pot dishes, such as *[ričet](/wiki/Ri%C4%8Det "Ričet")*, [Istrian stew](/wiki/Istrian_stew "Istrian stew") (*jota*), [minestrone](/wiki/Minestrone "Minestrone") (*mineštra*), and *[žganci](/wiki/%C5%BDganci "Žganci")* buckwheat spoonbread; in the [Prekmurje](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje") region there is also *[bujta repa](/wiki/Bujta_repa "Bujta repa")*, and *[prekmurska gibanica](/wiki/Prekmurska_gibanica "Prekmurska gibanica")* pastry. [Prosciutto](/wiki/Prosciutto "Prosciutto") (*pršut*) is a delicacy of the [Slovene Littoral](/wiki/Slovene_Littoral "Slovene Littoral"). The *[potica](/wiki/Potica "Potica")* (a type of [nut roll](/wiki/Nut_roll "Nut roll")) has become a symbol of Slovenia, especially among the [Slovene diaspora in the United States](/wiki/American_Slovenes "American Slovenes"). [Soups](/wiki/Soup "Soup") were added to the traditional one-pot meals and various kinds of porridge and stew only in relatively recent history.

Each year since 2000, the [Roasted Potato Festival](/w/index.php?title=Roasted_Potato_Festival&action=edit&redlink=1 "Roasted Potato Festival (page does not exist)") has been organized by the *Society for the Recognition of Roasted Potatoes as a Distinct Dish*. Roasted potatoes, which have been traditionally served in most Slovenian families only on Sundays, have been depicted on a special edition of [post marks](/wiki/Post_mark "Post mark") by the [Post of Slovenia](/wiki/Post_of_Slovenia "Post of Slovenia") in 2012.[[284]](#cite_note-287) The best known sausage is *[kranjska klobasa](/wiki/Kranjska_klobasa "Kranjska klobasa")*. Slovenia is also the home of the world's [oldest vine](/wiki/Old_vine "Old vine"), which is 400 years old.

Slovenia has been awarded the [European Region of Gastronomy](/wiki/European_Region_of_Gastronomy "European Region of Gastronomy") title for the year 2021.[[285]](#cite_note-288)

### Dance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Dance")]

Historically the most notable Slovenian ballet dancers and choreographers were [Pino Mlakar](/wiki/Pino_Mlakar "Pino Mlakar"),[[286]](#cite_note-289) who in 1927 graduated from the [Rudolf Laban](/wiki/Rudolf_von_Laban "Rudolf von Laban") Choreographic Institute, and there met his future wife, balerina [Maria Luiza Pia Beatrice Scholz](/wiki/Pia_Mlakar "Pia Mlakar"). Together they worked as a leading dancer and a choreographer in [Dessau](/wiki/Dessau "Dessau"), [Zürich](/wiki/Z%C3%BCrich "Zürich"), and State opera in [München](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen "München").[[287]](#cite_note-Opera_si-290) Their plan to build a Slovenian dance centre at [Rožnik Hill](/wiki/Ro%C5%BEnik_Hill "Rožnik Hill") after the World War II was supported by the minister of culture, Ferdo Kozak, but was cancelled by his successor.[[288]](#cite_note-291) Pino Mlakar was also a full professor at the [Academy for Theatre, Radio, Film and Television](/wiki/Academy_for_Theatre,_Radio,_Film_and_Television "Academy for Theatre, Radio, Film and Television") (AGRFT) of the [University of Ljubljana](/wiki/University_of_Ljubljana "University of Ljubljana"). A [Mary Wigman](/wiki/Mary_Wigman "Mary Wigman") [modern dance](/wiki/Modern_dance "Modern dance") school was founded in the 1930s by her student, [Meta Vidmar](/wiki/Meta_Vidmar "Meta Vidmar"), in Ljubljana.[[289]](#cite_note-292)

### Festivals, book fairs, and other events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Festivals, book fairs, and other events")]

A number of music, theatre, film, book, and children's festivals take place in Slovenia each year, including the music festivals [Ljubljana Summer Festival](/wiki/Ljubljana_Summer_Festival "Ljubljana Summer Festival") and [Lent Festival](/wiki/Lent_Festival "Lent Festival"), the stand-up comedy [Punch Festival](/wiki/Punch_Festival "Punch Festival"), the children's [Pippi Longstocking Festival](/w/index.php?title=Pippi_Longstocking_Festival&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pippi Longstocking Festival (page does not exist)"), and the book festivals [Slovene book fair](/wiki/Slovene_book_fair "Slovene book fair") and [Frankfurt after the Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt_after_the_Frankfurt "Frankfurt after the Frankfurt").

The most notable music festival of Slovene music was historically the [Slovenska popevka](/wiki/Slovenska_popevka "Slovenska popevka") festival.[[290]](#cite_note-293) Between 1981 and 2000 the [Novi Rock](/wiki/Novi_Rock "Novi Rock") festival was notable for bringing rock music across [Iron curtain](/wiki/Iron_curtain "Iron curtain") from the West to the Slovenian and then Yugoslav audience. The long tradition of jazz festivals in [Titoist](/wiki/Titoism "Titoism") Yugoslavia began with the Ljubljana Jazz Festival which has been held annually in Slovenia since 1960.[[291]](#cite_note-294)

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Film")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Ptuj%2C_city_cinema.jpg/250px-Ptuj%2C_city_cinema.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ptuj,_city_cinema.jpg)

[Ptuj City Cinema](/wiki/Ptuj_City_Cinema "Ptuj City Cinema") (from 3 March 1897) is the oldest still active commercial (tickets) movie theater in the world.[[292]](#cite_note-Ptuj1-295)[[293]](#cite_note-Ptuj2-296)[[294]](#cite_note-Ptuj3-297)

Slovene film actors and actresses historically include [Ida Kravanja](/wiki/Ida_Kravanja "Ida Kravanja"), who played her roles as *Ita Rina* in the early European films, and [Metka Bučar](/w/index.php?title=Metka_Bu%C4%8Dar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Metka Bučar (page does not exist)").[[295]](#cite_note-298) After WW II, one of the most notable film actors was [Polde Bibič](/wiki/Polde_Bibi%C4%8D "Polde Bibič"), who played a number of roles in many films that were well received in Slovenia, and also performed in television and radio drama.[[296]](#cite_note-Bibic_in_memoriam-299)

Feature film and short film production in Slovenia historically includes [Karol Grossmann](/wiki/Karol_Grossmann "Karol Grossmann"), [František Čap](/wiki/Franti%C5%A1ek_%C4%8Cap "František Čap"), [France Štiglic](/wiki/France_%C5%A0tiglic "France Štiglic"), [Igor Pretnar](/wiki/Igor_Pretnar "Igor Pretnar"), [Jože Pogačnik](/wiki/Jo%C5%BEe_Poga%C4%8Dnik "Jože Pogačnik"), [Peter Zobec](/w/index.php?title=Peter_Zobec&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peter Zobec (page does not exist)"), [Matjaž Klopčič](/wiki/Matja%C5%BE_Klop%C4%8Di%C4%8D "Matjaž Klopčič"), [Boštjan Hladnik](/wiki/Bo%C5%A1tjan_Hladnik "Boštjan Hladnik"), [Dušan Jovanović](/wiki/Du%C5%A1an_Jovanovi%C4%87_(theatre_director) "Dušan Jovanović (theatre director)"), [Vitan Mal](/wiki/Vitan_Mal "Vitan Mal"), [Franci Slak](/wiki/Franci_Slak "Franci Slak"), and [Karpo Godina](/wiki/Karpo_Godina "Karpo Godina") as its most established filmmakers. Contemporary film directors [Filip Robar - Dorin](/wiki/Filip_Robar_-_Dorin "Filip Robar - Dorin"), [Jan Cvitkovič](/wiki/Jan_Cvitkovi%C4%8D "Jan Cvitkovič"), [Damjan Kozole](/wiki/Damjan_Kozole "Damjan Kozole"), [Janez Lapajne](/wiki/Janez_Lapajne "Janez Lapajne"), [Mitja Okorn](/wiki/Mitja_Okorn "Mitja Okorn"), and [Marko Naberšnik](/wiki/Marko_Naber%C5%A1nik "Marko Naberšnik") are among the representatives of the so-called "Renaissance of Slovenian cinema". Slovene screenwriters, who are not film directors, include [Saša Vuga](/w/index.php?title=Sa%C5%A1a_Vuga&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saša Vuga (page does not exist)") and [Miha Mazzini](/wiki/Miha_Mazzini "Miha Mazzini"). Women film directors include [Polona Sepe](/w/index.php?title=Polona_Sepe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Polona Sepe (page does not exist)"), [Hanna A. W. Slak](/w/index.php?title=Hanna_A._W._Slak&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hanna A. W. Slak (page does not exist)"), and [Maja Weiss](/w/index.php?title=Maja_Weiss&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maja Weiss (page does not exist)").[[297]](#cite_note-300)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Literature")]

Further information: [List of Slovene writers and poets in Hungary](/wiki/List_of_Slovene_writers_and_poets_in_Hungary "List of Slovene writers and poets in Hungary") and [List of Slovenian women writers](/wiki/List_of_Slovenian_women_writers "List of Slovenian women writers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Pre%C5%A1ern-Goldenstein.jpg/250px-Pre%C5%A1ern-Goldenstein.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pre%C5%A1ern-Goldenstein.jpg)

[France Prešeren](/wiki/France_Pre%C5%A1eren "France Prešeren"), best-known Slovenian poet

The history of [Slovene literature](/wiki/Slovene_literature "Slovene literature") began in the 16th century with [Primož Trubar](/wiki/Primo%C5%BE_Trubar "Primož Trubar") and [other Protestant Reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation_in_the_Slovene_Lands "Protestant Reformation in the Slovene Lands"). Poetry in [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language") achieved its highest level with the [Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_poetry "Romantic poetry") poet [France Prešeren](/wiki/France_Pre%C5%A1eren "France Prešeren"). In the 20th century, Slovene literary fiction went through several periods: the beginning of the century was marked by the authors of the [Slovene Modernism](/w/index.php?title=Slovene_Modernism&action=edit&redlink=1 "Slovene Modernism (page does not exist)"), with the most influential Slovene writer and playwright, [Ivan Cankar](/wiki/Ivan_Cankar "Ivan Cankar"); it was then followed by [expressionism](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism") ([Srečko Kosovel](/wiki/Sre%C4%8Dko_Kosovel "Srečko Kosovel")), avantgardism ([Anton Podbevšek](/wiki/Anton_Podbev%C5%A1ek "Anton Podbevšek"), [Ferdo Delak](/wiki/Ferdo_Delak "Ferdo Delak")) and [social realism](/wiki/Social_realism "Social realism") ([Ciril Kosmač](/wiki/Ciril_Kosma%C4%8D "Ciril Kosmač"), [Prežihov Voranc](/wiki/Pre%C5%BEihov_Voranc "Prežihov Voranc")) before World War II, the [poetry of resistance and revolution](/w/index.php?title=Poetry_of_resistance_and_revolution&action=edit&redlink=1 "Poetry of resistance and revolution (page does not exist)") ([Karel Destovnik Kajuh](/wiki/Karel_Destovnik_Kajuh "Karel Destovnik Kajuh"), [Matej Bor](/wiki/Matej_Bor "Matej Bor")) during the war, and [intimism](/wiki/Intimism_(Slovene_poetry) "Intimism (Slovene poetry)") ([Poems of the Four](/w/index.php?title=Poems_of_the_Four&action=edit&redlink=1 "Poems of the Four (page does not exist)"), 1953), [post-war modernism](/w/index.php?title=Slovenian_post-war_modernism&action=edit&redlink=1 "Slovenian post-war modernism (page does not exist)") ([Edvard Kocbek](/wiki/Edvard_Kocbek "Edvard Kocbek")), and [existentialism](/wiki/Existentialism "Existentialism") ([Dane Zajc](/wiki/Dane_Zajc "Dane Zajc")) after the war.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Postmodernist](/wiki/Postmodernism "Postmodernism") authors include [Boris A. Novak](/wiki/Boris_A._Novak "Boris A. Novak"), [Marko Kravos](/wiki/Marko_Kravos "Marko Kravos"), [Drago Jančar](/wiki/Drago_Jan%C4%8Dar "Drago Jančar"), [Evald Flisar](/wiki/Evald_Flisar "Evald Flisar"), [Tomaž Šalamun](/wiki/Toma%C5%BE_%C5%A0alamun "Tomaž Šalamun"), and [Brina Svit](/wiki/Brina_Svit "Brina Svit"). Among the post-1990 authors best known are [Aleš Debeljak](/wiki/Ale%C5%A1_Debeljak "Aleš Debeljak"), [Miha Mazzini](/wiki/Miha_Mazzini "Miha Mazzini"), and [Alojz Ihan](/wiki/Alojz_Ihan "Alojz Ihan"). There are several [literary magazines](/wiki/Literary_magazines "Literary magazines") that publish Slovene prose, poetry, essays, and local literary criticism.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Today, notable authors include [Slavoj Žižek](/wiki/Slavoj_%C5%BDi%C5%BEek "Slavoj Žižek"), [Mladen Dolar](/wiki/Mladen_Dolar "Mladen Dolar"), [Alenka Zupančič](/wiki/Alenka_Zupan%C4%8Di%C4%8D "Alenka Zupančič") as well as [Boris Pahor](/wiki/Boris_Pahor "Boris Pahor").[[298]](#cite_note-301)[[299]](#cite_note-302)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media of Slovenia](/wiki/Media_of_Slovenia "Media of Slovenia")

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of Slovenia](/wiki/Music_of_Slovenia "Music of Slovenia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Pre%C4%8Drtana_kitica_Zdravljice.jpg/250px-Pre%C4%8Drtana_kitica_Zdravljice.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pre%C4%8Drtana_kitica_Zdravljice.jpg)

"[Zdravljica](/wiki/Zdravljica "Zdravljica")" (*A Toast*; part) with rejection mark from [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") censorship (due to potential revolutionary content); the music of Zdravljica is now the Slovenian national [anthem](/wiki/Anthem "Anthem")

Music of Slovenia historically includes numerous musicians and composers, such as the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") composer [Jacobus Gallus](/wiki/Jacobus_Gallus "Jacobus Gallus"), who greatly influenced Central European classical music, the Baroque composer [Joannes Baptista Dolar](/wiki/Joannes_Baptista_Dolar "Joannes Baptista Dolar"), and the violin virtuoso [Giuseppe Tartini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Tartini "Giuseppe Tartini").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The first Slovenian hymnal, *[Eni Psalmi](/w/index.php?title=Eni_Psalmi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eni Psalmi (page does not exist)")*, was published in 1567. This period saw the rise of musicians like [Jacobus Gallus](/wiki/Jacobus_Gallus "Jacobus Gallus") and [George Slatkonia](/wiki/George_Slatkonia "George Slatkonia").[[300]](#cite_note-303) In 1701, [Johann Berthold von Höffer](/wiki/Johann_Berthold_von_H%C3%B6ffer "Johann Berthold von Höffer") founded the [Academia Philharmonicorum Labacensis](/wiki/Slovenian_Philharmonics "Slovenian Philharmonics"), as one of the oldest such institutions in Europe, based on Italian models.[[301]](#cite_note-304) Composers of Slovenian [Lieder](/wiki/Lieder "Lieder") and [art songs](/wiki/Art_song "Art song") include [Emil Adamič](/wiki/Emil_Adami%C4%8D "Emil Adamič"), [Fran Gerbič](/wiki/Fran_Gerbi%C4%8D "Fran Gerbič"), [Alojz Geržinič](/wiki/Alojz_Ger%C5%BEini%C4%8D "Alojz Geržinič"), [Benjamin Ipavec](/wiki/Benjamin_Ipavec "Benjamin Ipavec"), [Davorin Jenko](/wiki/Davorin_Jenko "Davorin Jenko"), [Anton Lajovic](/wiki/Anton_Lajovic "Anton Lajovic"), [Kamilo Mašek](/wiki/Kamilo_Ma%C5%A1ek "Kamilo Mašek"), [Josip Pavčič](/wiki/Josip_Pav%C4%8Di%C4%8D "Josip Pavčič"), [Zorko Prelovec](/wiki/Zorko_Prelovec "Zorko Prelovec"), and [Lucijan Marija Škerjanc](/wiki/Lucijan_Marija_%C5%A0kerjanc "Lucijan Marija Škerjanc").

In the early 20th century, [impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") was spreading across Slovenia, which soon produced composers [Marij Kogoj](/wiki/Marij_Kogoj "Marij Kogoj") and [Slavko Osterc](/wiki/Slavko_Osterc "Slavko Osterc"). Avant-garde [classical music](/wiki/European_classical_music "European classical music") arose in Slovenia in the 1960s, largely due to the work of [Uroš Krek](/wiki/Uro%C5%A1_Krek "Uroš Krek"), [Dane Škerl](/w/index.php?title=Dane_Skerl&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dane Skerl (page does not exist)"), [Primož Ramovš](/wiki/Primoz_Ramovs "Primoz Ramovs") and [Ivo Petrić](/wiki/Ivo_Petric "Ivo Petric"), who also conducted the [Slavko Osterc Ensemble](/wiki/Slavko_Osterc_Ensemble "Slavko Osterc Ensemble"). [Jakob Jež](/w/index.php?title=Jakob_Jez&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jakob Jez (page does not exist)"), [Darijan Božič](/w/index.php?title=Darijan_Bo%C5%BEi%C4%8D&action=edit&redlink=1 "Darijan Božič (page does not exist)"), [Lojze Lebič](/w/index.php?title=Lojze_Lebic&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lojze Lebic (page does not exist)") and [Vinko Globokar](/wiki/Vinko_Globokar "Vinko Globokar") have since composed enduring works, especially Globokar's *[L'Armonia](/w/index.php?title=L%27Armonia_(opera)&action=edit&redlink=1 "L'Armonia (opera) (page does not exist)")*, an opera.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Modern composers include [Uroš Rojko](/w/index.php?title=Uros_Rojko&action=edit&redlink=1 "Uros Rojko (page does not exist)"), [Tomaž Svete](/w/index.php?title=Tomaz_Svete&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tomaz Svete (page does not exist)"), [Brina Jež-Brezavšček](/w/index.php?title=Brina_Jez-Brezavscek&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brina Jez-Brezavscek (page does not exist)"), [Božidar Kantušer](/wiki/Bo%C5%BEidar_Kantu%C5%A1er "Božidar Kantušer") and [Aldo Kumar](/w/index.php?title=Aldo_Kumar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Aldo Kumar (page does not exist)").

The [Slovene National Opera and Ballet Theatre](/wiki/Slovene_National_Opera_and_Ballet_Theatre "Slovene National Opera and Ballet Theatre") serves as the national opera and ballet house. The [Slovenian Philharmonics](/wiki/Slovenian_Philharmonics "Slovenian Philharmonics"), established in 1701 as part of [Academia operosorum Labacensis](/wiki/Academia_operosorum_Labacensis "Academia operosorum Labacensis"), is among the oldest such institutions in Europe.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Slak_lojze.jpg/250px-Slak_lojze.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slak_lojze.jpg)

Folk musician [Lojze Slak](/wiki/Lojze_Slak "Lojze Slak")

[Harmony](/wiki/Harmony "Harmony") singing is a deep rooted tradition in Slovenia, and is at least three-part singing (four voices), while in some regions even up to eight-part singing (nine voices). Slovenian folk songs, thus, usually resounds soft and harmonious, and are very seldom in minor. Traditional Slovenian folk music is performed on Styrian harmonica (the oldest type of accordion), fiddle, clarinet, [zithers](/wiki/Zither "Zither"), flute, and by brass bands of alpine type. In [eastern Slovenia](/wiki/Prekmurje "Prekmurje"), fiddle and cimbalon bands are called [velike goslarije](/w/index.php?title=Velike_goslarije&action=edit&redlink=1 "Velike goslarije (page does not exist)"). From 1952 on, the [Slavko Avsenik](/wiki/Slavko_Avsenik "Slavko Avsenik")'s band began to appear in broadcasts, movies, and concerts all over the [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany"), inventing the original "[Oberkrainer](/wiki/Gorenjska "Gorenjska")" country sound. The band produced nearly 1000 original compositions, an integral part of the [Slovenian-style polka](/wiki/Slovenian-style_polka "Slovenian-style polka") legacy. Many musicians followed Avsenik's steps, including [Lojze Slak](/wiki/Lojze_Slak "Lojze Slak").[[302]](#cite_note-305)[[303]](#cite_note-306)[[304]](#cite_note-307)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/LAIBACH_Press_Photo_2011.jpg/250px-LAIBACH_Press_Photo_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:LAIBACH_Press_Photo_2011.jpg)

The [industrial](/wiki/Industrial_music "Industrial music") group [Laibach](/wiki/Laibach_(band) "Laibach (band)")

Among pop, rock, industrial, and indie musicians the most popular in Slovenia include [industrial music](/wiki/Industrial_music "Industrial music") group [Laibach](/wiki/Laibach_(band) "Laibach (band)"), as well as [Siddharta](/wiki/Siddharta_(band) "Siddharta (band)"), a rock band formed in 1995. [Perpetuum Jazzile](/wiki/Perpetuum_Jazzile "Perpetuum Jazzile") is the group from Slovenia that is internationally most listened online, with more than 23 million views for the official [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") "[Africa](/wiki/Africa_(song) "Africa (song)")" video since its publishing on YouTube in May 2009 (through January 2023).[[305]](#cite_note-youtube_africa-308) Other Slovenian bands include a historically [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock") ones that were also popular in [Titoist](/wiki/Titoism "Titoism") Yugoslavia, such as [Buldožer](/wiki/Buldo%C5%BEer "Buldožer") and [Lačni Franz](/wiki/La%C4%8Dni_Franz "Lačni Franz"), which inspired later [comedy rock](/wiki/Comedy_rock "Comedy rock") bands including [Zmelkoow](/wiki/Zmelkoow "Zmelkoow"), [Slon in Sadež](/wiki/Slon_in_Sade%C5%BE "Slon in Sadež") and [Mi2](/wiki/Mi2_(rock_band) "Mi2 (rock band)").[[306]](#cite_note-309) With exception of [Terrafolk](/wiki/Terrafolk "Terrafolk") that made appearances worldwide, other bands, such as Avtomobili, [Zaklonišče Prepeva](/wiki/Zakloni%C5%A1%C4%8De_Prepeva "Zaklonišče Prepeva"), [Šank Rock](/wiki/%C5%A0ank_Rock "Šank Rock"), [Big Foot Mama](/wiki/Big_Foot_Mama "Big Foot Mama"), [Dan D](/wiki/Dan_D "Dan D"), and [Zablujena generacija](/wiki/Zablujena_generacija "Zablujena generacija"), are mostly unknown outside the country. Slovenian metal bands include [Noctiferia](/w/index.php?title=Noctiferia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Noctiferia (page does not exist)") ([death metal](/wiki/Death_metal "Death metal")), [Negligence](/wiki/Negligence_(band) "Negligence (band)") ([thrash metal](/wiki/Thrash_metal "Thrash metal")), [Naio Ssaion](/wiki/Naio_Ssaion "Naio Ssaion") ([gothic metal](/wiki/Gothic_metal "Gothic metal")), and [Within Destruction](/wiki/Within_Destruction "Within Destruction") ([deathcore](/wiki/Deathcore "Deathcore")).[[307]](#cite_note-310)
Slovenian post-WWII singer-songwriters include [Frane Milčinski](/wiki/Frane_Mil%C4%8Dinski "Frane Milčinski"), [Tomaž Pengov](/wiki/Toma%C5%BE_Pengov "Tomaž Pengov") whose 1973 album *Odpotovanja* is considered to be the first singer-songwriter album in [former Yugoslavia](/wiki/Former_Yugoslavia "Former Yugoslavia"),[[308]](#cite_note-311) [Tomaž Domicelj](/w/index.php?title=Toma%C5%BE_Domicelj&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tomaž Domicelj (page does not exist)"), [Marko Brecelj](/wiki/Marko_Brecelj "Marko Brecelj"), [Andrej Šifrer](/w/index.php?title=Andrej_%C5%A0ifrer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Andrej Šifrer (page does not exist)"), [Eva Sršen](/wiki/Eva_Sr%C5%A1en "Eva Sršen"), [Neca Falk](/wiki/Neca_Falk "Neca Falk"), and [Jani Kovačič](/wiki/Jani_Kova%C4%8Di%C4%8D "Jani Kovačič"). After 1990, [Adi Smolar](/wiki/Adi_Smolar "Adi Smolar"), [Iztok Mlakar](/wiki/Iztok_Mlakar "Iztok Mlakar"), [Vita Mavrič](/wiki/Vita_Mavri%C4%8D "Vita Mavrič"), [Vlado Kreslin](/wiki/Vlado_Kreslin "Vlado Kreslin"), [Zoran Predin](/wiki/Zoran_Predin "Zoran Predin"), [Peter Lovšin](/wiki/Peter_Lov%C5%A1in "Peter Lovšin"), and [Magnifico](/wiki/Magnifico_(musician) "Magnifico (musician)") have been popular in Slovenia, as well. In the 21st century, there have been many successful artists from Slovenia. They include country musician [Manu](/wiki/ManuElla "ManuElla"), [zalagasper](/wiki/Zalagasper "Zalagasper"), [Nika Zorjan](/wiki/Nika_Zorjan "Nika Zorjan"), [Omar Naber](/wiki/Omar_Naber "Omar Naber"), [Raiven](/wiki/Raiven "Raiven"), and [Joker Out](/wiki/Joker_Out "Joker Out").[[309]](#cite_note-312)

### Theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Theatre")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Drama_theatre_in_Ljubljana%2C_2011.jpg/250px-Drama_theatre_in_Ljubljana%2C_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drama_theatre_in_Ljubljana,_2011.jpg)

The [National Theatre](/wiki/Slovene_National_Theatre,_Ljubljana "Slovene National Theatre, Ljubljana") in [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana")

Theater has a rich tradition in Slovenia, starting with the 1867 first ever Slovene-language drama performance.[[310]](#cite_note-313) In addition to the main houses, which include [Slovene National Theatre, Ljubljana](/wiki/Slovene_National_Theatre,_Ljubljana "Slovene National Theatre, Ljubljana") and [Maribor National Drama Theatre](/wiki/Maribor_National_Drama_Theatre "Maribor National Drama Theatre"), a number of small producers are active in Slovenia, including [physical theatre](/wiki/Physical_theatre "Physical theatre") (e.g. [Betontanc](/w/index.php?title=Betontanc&action=edit&redlink=1 "Betontanc (page does not exist)")), [street theatre](/wiki/Street_theatre "Street theatre") (e.g. [Ana Monró Theatre](/w/index.php?title=Ana_Monr%C3%B3_Theatre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ana Monró Theatre (page does not exist)")), [theatresports](/wiki/Theatresports "Theatresports") championship [Impro League](/wiki/Impro_League "Impro League"), and [improvisational theatre](/wiki/Improvisational_theatre "Improvisational theatre") (e.g. [IGLU Theatre](/wiki/IGLU_Theatre "IGLU Theatre")). A popular form is [puppetry](/wiki/Puppetry "Puppetry"), mainly performed in the [Ljubljana Puppet Theatre](/wiki/Ljubljana_Puppet_Theatre "Ljubljana Puppet Theatre").

### Visual arts, architecture and design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Visual arts, architecture and design")]

Slovenia's visual arts, architecture, and design are shaped by a number of architects, designers, painters, sculptors, photographers, graphics artists, as well as comics, illustration and conceptual artists. Two significant prestigious institutions exhibiting works of Slovene visual artists are the [National Gallery of Slovenia](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Slovenia "National Gallery of Slovenia") and the [Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art,_Ljubljana "Museum of Modern Art, Ljubljana").

[Modern architecture](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture") in Slovenia was introduced by [Max Fabiani](/wiki/Max_Fabiani "Max Fabiani"), and in the mid-war period, [Jože Plečnik](/wiki/Jo%C5%BEe_Ple%C4%8Dnik "Jože Plečnik") and [Ivan Vurnik](/wiki/Ivan_Vurnik "Ivan Vurnik").[[311]](#cite_note-Štravs2011-314) In the second half of the 20th century, the national and universal style were merged by the architects [Edvard Ravnikar](/wiki/Edvard_Ravnikar "Edvard Ravnikar") and first generation of his students: Milan Mihelič, Stanko Kristl, Savin Sever. The next generation is mainly still active, including [Marko Mušič](/wiki/Marko_Mu%C5%A1i%C4%8D "Marko Mušič"), [Vojteh Ravnikar](/wiki/Vojteh_Ravnikar "Vojteh Ravnikar"), and [Jurij Kobe](/w/index.php?title=Jurij_Kobe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jurij Kobe (page does not exist)"). Selected works of Jože Plečnik which shaped Ljubljana during the inter-war period were inscribed on [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s list of [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 2021.[[312]](#cite_note-plecnik_whc-315)

A number of [conceptual visual art](/wiki/Conceptual_art "Conceptual art") groups formed, including [OHO](/w/index.php?title=OHO_(art_group)&action=edit&redlink=1 "OHO (art group) (page does not exist)"), [Group 69](/w/index.php?title=Group_69&action=edit&redlink=1 "Group 69 (page does not exist)"), and [IRWIN](/wiki/IRWIN "IRWIN"). Nowadays, the Slovene visual arts are diverse, based on tradition, reflect the influence of neighboring nations and are intertwined with modern European movements.[[313]](#cite_note-316)

Internationally most notable Slovenian design items include the 1952 [Rex](/wiki/Rex_(chair) "Rex (chair)") chair, a [Scandinavian design](/wiki/Scandinavian_design "Scandinavian design")-inspired wooden chair, by interior designer [Niko Kralj](/w/index.php?title=Niko_Kralj&action=edit&redlink=1 "Niko Kralj (page does not exist)") that was given in 2012 a permanent place in [Designmuseum](/w/index.php?title=Designmuseum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Designmuseum (page does not exist)"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), the largest museum of design in Scandinavia, and is included in the collection of the [Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art,_New_York "Museum of Modern Art, New York") [MOMA](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art "Museum of Modern Art") in New York City, as well. An [industrial design](/wiki/Industrial_design "Industrial design") item that has changed the international ski industry is [Elan SCX](/wiki/Elan_SCX "Elan SCX") by [Elan](/wiki/Elan_(company) "Elan (company)") company.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/V._Vodnik_monument_Ljubljana_front_side_2008-03.jpg/250px-V._Vodnik_monument_Ljubljana_front_side_2008-03.jpg)](/wiki/File:V._Vodnik_monument_Ljubljana_front_side_2008-03.jpg)

The sculpture of the poet [Valentin Vodnik](/wiki/Valentin_Vodnik "Valentin Vodnik") was created by Alojz Gangl in 1889 as part of [Vodnik Monument](/wiki/Vodnik_Monument "Vodnik Monument"), the first Slovene national monument.

The renewal of Slovene sculpture begun with [Alojz Gangl](/w/index.php?title=Alojz_Gangl&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alojz Gangl (page does not exist)") who created sculptures for the public monuments of the [Carniolan](/wiki/Carniola "Carniola") polymath [Johann Weikhard von Valvasor](/wiki/Johann_Weikhard_von_Valvasor "Johann Weikhard von Valvasor") and [Valentin Vodnik](/wiki/Valentin_Vodnik "Valentin Vodnik"), the first Slovene poet and journalist, as well as *The Genius of the Theatre* and other statues for the [Slovenian National Opera and Ballet Theatre](/wiki/Slovenian_National_Opera_and_Ballet_Theatre "Slovenian National Opera and Ballet Theatre") building.[[314]](#cite_note-317) The development of sculpture after World War II was led by a number of artists, including brothers [Boris](/wiki/Boris_Kalin "Boris Kalin") and [Zdenko Kalin](/w/index.php?title=Zdenko_Kalin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zdenko Kalin (page does not exist)"), [Jakob Savinšek](/wiki/Jakob_Savin%C5%A1ek "Jakob Savinšek") stayed with figural art. Younger sculptors, for example [Janez Boljka](/w/index.php?title=Janez_Boljka&action=edit&redlink=1 "Janez Boljka (page does not exist)"), [Drago Tršar](/w/index.php?title=Drago_Tr%C5%A1ar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Drago Tršar (page does not exist)") and particularly [Slavko Tihec](/w/index.php?title=Slavko_Tihec&action=edit&redlink=1 "Slavko Tihec (page does not exist)"), moved towards [abstract](/wiki/Abstract_sculpture "Abstract sculpture") forms. [Jakov Brdar](/wiki/Jakov_Brdar "Jakov Brdar") and [Mirsad Begić](/w/index.php?title=Mirsad_Begi%C4%87&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mirsad Begić (page does not exist)") returned to human figures.

During World War II, numerous graphics were created by [Božidar Jakac](/wiki/Bo%C5%BEidar_Jakac "Božidar Jakac"), who helped establish the post-war [Academy of Visual Arts](/wiki/Academy_of_Visual_Arts_and_Design,_Ljubljana "Academy of Visual Arts and Design, Ljubljana") in Ljubljana.

In 1917 [Hinko Smrekar](/wiki/Hinko_Smrekar "Hinko Smrekar") illustrated [Fran Levstik](/wiki/Fran_Levstik "Fran Levstik")'s book about the well-known Slovene folk hero [Martin Krpan](/wiki/Martin_Krpan "Martin Krpan"). The children's books illustrators include a number of women illustrators, such as [Marlenka Stupica](/wiki/Marlenka_Stupica "Marlenka Stupica"), [Marija Lucija Stupica](/wiki/Marija_Lucija_Stupica "Marija Lucija Stupica"), [Ančka Gošnik Godec](/wiki/An%C4%8Dka_Go%C5%A1nik_Godec "Ančka Gošnik Godec"), [Marjanca Jemec Božič](/wiki/Marjanca_Jemec_Bo%C5%BEi%C4%8D "Marjanca Jemec Božič"), and [Jelka Reichman](/wiki/Jelka_Reichman "Jelka Reichman").

Historically, painting and sculpture in Slovenia was in the late 18th and the 19th century marked by [Neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") ([Matevž Langus](/w/index.php?title=Matev%C5%BE_Langus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Matevž Langus (page does not exist)")), [Biedermeier](/wiki/Biedermeier#Visual_arts "Biedermeier") ([Giuseppe Tominz](/wiki/Giuseppe_Tominz "Giuseppe Tominz")) and [Romanticism](/wiki/Romantic_visual_arts "Romantic visual arts") ([Michael Stroy](/wiki/Michael_Stroy "Michael Stroy")). The first art exhibition in Slovenia was organized in the late 19th century by [Ivana Kobilca](/wiki/Ivana_Kobilca "Ivana Kobilca"). [Impressionist](/wiki/Impressionism_(arts) "Impressionism (arts)") artists include [Matej Sternen](/wiki/Matej_Sternen "Matej Sternen"), [Matija Jama](/wiki/Matija_Jama "Matija Jama"), [Rihard Jakopič](/wiki/Rihard_Jakopi%C4%8D "Rihard Jakopič"), [Ivan Grohar](/wiki/Ivan_Grohar "Ivan Grohar") whose *The Sower* (Slovene: Sejalec) was depicted on the €0.05 [Slovenian euro coins](/wiki/Slovenian_euro_coins "Slovenian euro coins"), and [Franc Berneker](/wiki/Franc_Berneker "Franc Berneker"), who introduced impressionism to Slovenia. [Espressionist](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism") painters include [Veno Pilon](/wiki/Veno_Pilon "Veno Pilon") and [Tone Kralj](/wiki/Tone_Kralj "Tone Kralj") whose picture book, reprinted thirteen times, is now the most recognisable image of Martin Krpan.[[315]](#cite_note-318) Some of the best known painters in the second half of the 20th century were [Zoran Mušič](/wiki/Zoran_Mu%C5%A1i%C4%8D "Zoran Mušič"), [Gabrijel Stupica](/w/index.php?title=Gabrijel_Stupica&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gabrijel Stupica (page does not exist)") and [Marij Pregelj](/wiki/Marij_Pregelj "Marij Pregelj").

In 1841, [Janez Puhar](/wiki/Janez_Puhar "Janez Puhar") invented a process for photography on glass, recognized on 17 June 1852 in Paris by the Académie Nationale Agricole, Manufacturière et Commerciale.[[316]](#cite_note-319) [Gojmir Anton Kos](/wiki/Gojmir_Anton_Kos "Gojmir Anton Kos") was a notable [realist](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)") painter and photographer between the First and Second World Wars.

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slovenia&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sport in Slovenia](/wiki/Sport_in_Slovenia "Sport in Slovenia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Tina_Maze_with_Olympic_silver_medal_2010.jpg/250px-Tina_Maze_with_Olympic_silver_medal_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tina_Maze_with_Olympic_silver_medal_2010.jpg)

Alpine skier [Tina Maze](/wiki/Tina_Maze "Tina Maze"), a double Olympic gold medalist and the overall winner of the 2012–13 World Cup season

Slovenia is a natural sports venue, with many Slovenians actively practicing sports.[[317]](#cite_note-SportSLO.SI-320) A variety of sports are played in Slovenia on a professional level,[[318]](#cite_note-medjugorac4-321) with international successes in handball, basketball, volleyball, football, ice hockey, rowing, swimming, tennis, boxing, climbing, road cycling and athletics. Prior to World War II, gymnastics and fencing used to be the most popular sports in Slovenia, with athletes like [Leon Štukelj](/wiki/Leon_%C5%A0tukelj "Leon Štukelj") and [Miroslav Cerar](/wiki/Miroslav_Cerar_(gymnast) "Miroslav Cerar (gymnast)") gaining gold Olympic medals.[[319]](#cite_note-322) Football gained popularity in the interwar period. After 1945, basketball, handball and volleyball have become popular among Slovenians, and from the mid-1970s, winter sports as well. Since 1992, Slovenian sportspeople have won [59 Olympic medals](/wiki/Slovenia_at_the_Olympics "Slovenia at the Olympics"), including 16 gold medals, and [26 Paralympic medals](/wiki/Slovenia_at_the_Paralympics "Slovenia at the Paralympics") with five golds.[[320]](#cite_note-323)[[321]](#cite_note-324)

Individual sports are also very popular in Slovenia, including tennis and mountaineering, which are two of the most widespread sporting activities in Slovenia. Several Slovenian extreme and endurance sportsmen have gained an international reputation, including the mountaineer [Tomaž Humar](/wiki/Toma%C5%BE_Humar "Tomaž Humar"),[[322]](#cite_note-325) the mountain skier [Davo Karničar](/wiki/Davo_Karni%C4%8Dar "Davo Karničar"),[[323]](#cite_note-326) the ultramarathon swimmer [Martin Strel](/wiki/Martin_Strel "Martin Strel")[[324]](#cite_note-327) and the ultracyclist [Jure Robič](/wiki/Jure_Robi%C4%8D "Jure Robič").[[325]](#cite_note-328) Past and current winter sports athletes include alpine skiers, such as [Mateja Svet](/wiki/Mateja_Svet "Mateja Svet"), [Bojan Križaj](/wiki/Bojan_Kri%C5%BEaj "Bojan Križaj"), [Ilka Štuhec](/wiki/Ilka_%C5%A0tuhec "Ilka Štuhec") and double Olympic gold medalist [Tina Maze](/wiki/Tina_Maze "Tina Maze"),[[326]](#cite_note-329)[[327]](#cite_note-330) cross-country skier [Petra Majdič](/wiki/Petra_Majdi%C4%8D "Petra Majdič"),[[328]](#cite_note-331) and ski jumpers, such as [Primož Peterka](/wiki/Primo%C5%BE_Peterka "Primož Peterka") and [Peter Prevc](/wiki/Peter_Prevc "Peter Prevc").[[329]](#cite_note-332) Boxing has gained popularity since [Jan Zaveck](/wiki/Jan_Zaveck "Jan Zaveck") won the [IBF](/wiki/International_Boxing_Federation "International Boxing Federation") [Welterweight World Champion](/wiki/List_of_welterweight_boxing_champions "List of welterweight boxing champions") title in 2009.[[330]](#cite_note-333) In 2021, rock climber [Janja Garnbret](/wiki/Janja_Garnbret "Janja Garnbret") became the first female Olympic gold medalist in [sport climbing](/wiki/Sport_climbing "Sport climbing").[[331]](#cite_note-334)

In cycling, [Primož Roglič](/wiki/Primo%C5%BE_Rogli%C4%8D "Primož Roglič") became the first Slovenian to win a [Grand Tour](/wiki/Grand_Tour_(cycling) "Grand Tour (cycling)") when he won the [2019 Vuelta a España](/wiki/2019_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2019 Vuelta a España").[[332]](#cite_note-335) [Tadej Pogačar](/wiki/Tadej_Poga%C4%8Dar "Tadej Pogačar") won the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France"), the world's most competitive cycling race, in 2020, 2021, 2024, and 2025.[[333]](#cite_note-336) Prominent team sports in Slovenia include football, basketball, handball, volleyball, and ice hockey. The [men's national football team](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team") has qualified for two [European Championships](/wiki/UEFA_European_Football_Championship "UEFA European Football Championship") (2000 and 2024) and two [World Cups](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") (2002 and 2010).[[334]](#cite_note-337) Of Slovenian clubs, [NK Maribor](/wiki/NK_Maribor "NK Maribor") played three times in the group stages of the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League").[[335]](#cite_note-338) The [men's national basketball team](/wiki/Slovenian_national_basketball_team "Slovenian national basketball team") has participated at 15 [EuroBaskets](/wiki/EuroBasket "EuroBasket"), winning the gold medal in the 2017 edition,[[336]](#cite_note-339) and at four [FIBA World Championships](/wiki/FIBA_World_Championship "FIBA World Championship").[[337]](#cite_note-340) Slovenia also hosted the [EuroBasket 2013](/wiki/EuroBasket_2013 "EuroBasket 2013").[[338]](#cite_note-341) The [men's national handball team](/wiki/Slovenia_men%27s_national_handball_team "Slovenia men's national handball team") has qualified for four Olympics,[[339]](#cite_note-342) eleven IHF [World Championships](/wiki/IHF_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "IHF World Men's Handball Championship"), including their third-place finish in 2017,[[340]](#cite_note-343) and fifteen [European Championships](/wiki/European_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "European Men's Handball Championship").[[341]](#cite_note-344) Slovenia was the hosts of the [2004 European Championship](/wiki/2004_European_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "2004 European Men's Handball Championship"), where the national team won the silver medal.[[342]](#cite_note-2004handball-345) Slovenia's most prominent handball team, [RK Celje](/wiki/RK_Celje "RK Celje"), won the [EHF Champions League](/wiki/EHF_Champions_League "EHF Champions League") in the 2003–04 season.[[342]](#cite_note-2004handball-345) In women's handball, [RK Krim](/wiki/RK_Krim "RK Krim") won the [Champions League](/wiki/Women%27s_EHF_Champions_League "Women's EHF Champions League") in 2001 and 2003.[[343]](#cite_note-346) The [men's national volleyball team](/wiki/Slovenia_men%27s_national_volleyball_team "Slovenia men's national volleyball team") has won three silver medals at the [European Volleyball Championship](/wiki/Men%27s_European_Volleyball_Championship "Men's European Volleyball Championship"),[[344]](#cite_note-347) and finished fourth at the [2022 World Championship](/wiki/2022_FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "2022 FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship").[[345]](#cite_note-348) The [national ice hockey team](/wiki/Slovenia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Slovenia men's national ice hockey team") has participated in 31 [Ice Hockey World Championships](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships"), with 11 appearances in the highest division.[[346]](#cite_note-349)

Slovenia has hosted professional [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") events organized by [Brave Combat Federation](/wiki/Brave_Combat_Federation "Brave Combat Federation") partnering with WFC.[[347]](#cite_note-350)
